. AN EX-COLONIAL GOVERNOR
- ON EMIGRATION.
(From the Times )
: e
Sir Charles Du Cane, K.C.M.G., late
Governor of Tasmania, in distributing the
prizes at the Braxted Agricultural Society,
Essex, gave an account of the strange
sights be had secn in that Provinee, and
mentioned that on oue ocecasion a man
there came 800 miles to win a £SO prize
for ploughing. In mauy respectsit was a
charming country, but there are two siles
to every picture, and let them remember
before they went that it was not every
man who emigrated who could win £SO
prizes or become a farmer oun his own
account. The fact was that in all these
cases we heard a great deal about the suc
cessful men—and many are successful—
but we heard very little about the number
who did not succeed, and who found them
selves after a hard struggle to koep body
and soul together worse ofl Lau they
were here. Isa man was young, able
bodied, hones . thrifty, and inteliigent, no
doubt he had a chance of doing well for
himself, and of becoming independent.
But he would have to work quite as hard,
if not barder, than he ever did here, and
he had to run the risk of his health and
strength giving way under it and failing
just at the critical moment If he set
up farming ou his own account, he would
most probably have to live 1o a small hut
in the bush, far away from friends aud
neighbours DBut then it was said, “No
doubt the work is hard, but it is sweeten
ed with the bread of independence, and
a man can get meat to eat every day ;”
and that again was perfectly true. He
could tell them also that the Australian
colonies had thei: bad as well as their good
times 3 and when times were bad the la
bour market go! oversiocked and labour
ers were thrown out of employ just as
they were here in England. Just now in
New Zealand, New South Wales. and in
Tasmania able-bodied labour of all kinds
was in very great demand, and when he
was in New Zealand a year and a half ago,
A ratlway contractor told him he could not
ger men enough to cart ballast on his line
at Bs. per day. DBut only two or three
years before that time there were large
npumbers of able-bodied men in all the
Australian colonies destitute of employ
ment, and if any hicg were to happen to
send the price of woo suddenly down to
any greal extent, Mniost likely the same
thine would occur again. Now, he did
not say these things to discourage any
strong and able bodied young wmau who
bad set his heart on emigration from try
ing his fortune at it. Oun the countrary, he
only wished him to know that there was
shadow to be met with in the colonies as
well as sunshine, and if when he had bal
anced both sides of the account, he was
still desirous of emigrating, he should say
to him heartily, ¢ God speed you,” and
give him his best wishes for his rood for
tune. Above all, he wou'd say that when
a man is thoroughly unhappy and discon
tented with his lot in his mother evuntry,
the best thing he can do is to leave it.
Such a man would ¢ leave his country for
his couutry’s sood” in more seuses than
onc ; and let us hope he would do so for
his own gool also. But when a man 1s
happy and cooteuted at home he should
recommend him to stay athome, and, to
his mind, the same qualities which were
required to get a man on in the colonies
would in nine cases out of ten get him
on equally well at home.
DIFFICULTL.S OF MIXED"CULTI
VATION IN DEMERARA.
/ (From the Gétzcttq.), :
The speech of Mr. Russell when presid
-ing.-as President at the meeling of the
Royal Agricultural and Commercial Socie
ty on }oth Oct., issiguificant of the manner
in which men hitherto accustomed to luok
upon the cultivation of the sugar cane as
the only possible industry of a profitable
kind ‘which can be carried on in this
colony, are beginuing to recognize the
danger of adhering to this belief. Until
very reeently mixed cultivation was look-
$ T . ‘0 R O XAV 18-
ed upon as a favorite crochet of a few
theorists who, without possessing practi
cal knowledge of any kind, advocated
changes in the cultivation of the land al'o
gether regardless of the formidable diffi
culties that barred the road to success.
But Mr. Russell the leader lln so many
other enterprises having the prospeiity of
the colony for their ohject, has now bold
ly come forward as the champien of mix
ed cultivation, and has asked others to
join him in pressing upon the attention of
his fel ow-colouists the risk of relying upon
the cultivation «f the cane plant alone for
future prosperity. It does, indeed, seem
a strange anomaly that with a boundless
extent of fertile lund, it has hitherto, at
least during the last half century, been
cousidered impossible to cultivate upon a
large scile auy plant but that of the caune.
Yet this has been the case, nor is it at all
likely that for a leng:h of time to come
any different impression will be generally
received. And it is as nmpossible as it
would be unwise, to overlook the great
difliculties attendant upon any radical
change o the prive:ple of cultivation here.
Such a chunge of system can ouly be
brought sbout by slow and halting meaus,
and by practical proofs of the correctness of
the statements made by the advocates of
change.
Mr. Russell so fully recognizes this
most important fa t, he is so well aware
that men with capital at preseut invested
in sugar estates are not prepared jo trans
fer it to other ard possibly more
hazardous kinds of cultivation, and that
other capitalists are not, at all events
at present, likely to consent to embark
money in the cultivation of the products
which with us are usually classed under
the heading of mix.d cultivation that in
Lis speceh before the Agriculiural Society
on Thursday he mainly confined himseif
to urging the necessity of inducing the
peasants to cultivate more extensively
provisions, rice, &c This advice 18 no
doubt most wholesome, and 1t 18 much to
be desiied that it may be acted upon, but
Mr. Russell must be well aware that even
if his suggestion in this direction were
carried ioto effeet there would sl mwech
remain to be doue belore the prosperity
of the colony could be assured. It it is
ouce granted that the cave plant ought
not to absorb the entire attention of our
agriculturists, it must follow that until
they, the men of capital and intelligence,
recognize the neeessity of growing other
ptaats besides ibe caue all bas not been
done that should be done. And yet we
confess we have but little hope that uunder
existing circnmstarees any large amocunt
of capital will be diverted from the Sugar
listates On a small scale, much even
now may be, aud is being, done, but the
uncertainty ot a continued supply of la
bour frightens many from farniing estates
on the 1§1i1;(:ip!u of Mixed Cultivation. To
cultivate eoea, or rice, ortobaceco, and
similar producis profitably, a resident
peasantry, wi ling to woirk and work con
tinuoi sly for fair wages, is absolutely re
gquired. The complicated snd expeunsive
system by which sugar plasters obtain la
bour could not be adopted by men who
carvied out the priveipte of Mixed Culyi
vation. It would uct pay to adopt it, ex
cept on a very large scale. In some dis
tricts of the country there are indeed at
present small estates worked on the Mixed
Cultivation plan, and worked to a profit,
but these estates are of very limited area,
and whilst their present modest demands
on the labour market are honored, larger
ones might be refused. It would not pay
them to import immigrants from India;
they bave to remain dependent upon the
Creole population for labour; so that we
come to the conclusion that Mixed Cul
tivation on any large scale will only
become possible when agriculturists can
depend with certainty upon a steady
supply of native labour.
Thus we come back to the want so
long recognised.—a sufficiency of native
labour. 1t would be but wearisome to
recapitulate the reasons which will always
prevent this colony from being really
prosperous, in a tﬂﬂy, sound coundition,
until' this want is satisfied. But we may
ask what is being done to satisfy it? It
is a question of the greatest impurtance
to the colony, and yet a question very
difficult to answer satisfactorily. .Year
after year thousands of immigrants come
to our shores, but the large majority come
with the intention of leaving the colony
again after a while. What inducements
are offered them to remain? It is foolish
to suppose that the Indian coolie is so
passiovately wedded to his native land
as to determine to return, although he
might be much happier here. The ex
perience of the peighbouring colony of
Trinidad disproves this. There the coolies
are settling down in large numbers, and
the basis of that increase of resident la
bour so much required is being found.
Why cannot the coolie who comes hither
be induced to follow the example of his.
countryman in Trinidad ? Has any attempt ~
been made to persuade him to do so? It
would be difficult to answer this question
in the affirmative. We employ an expen
sive staff’ to procure labourers from India,
an expensive stafl to lock after and coddle
such labourers after they have arrived here ;
but what effirts are made, whose duty is
it, to endeavonr to induce them to remain
here altogether ¥ What has the Government
doune in this direction? What %#6uld it be
iu its power to do, did it sericusly buckle
itself to the work? Mr. Russell offered pri
z:s the other day for papers on different
subjects connecied with the management
ol sugar estates. Is there no other public
spirited and wealthy planter who will
offer similar inducements to colonists to
put their opinions on the above questions
on paper? No problegy is more mixed up
with the future of this colony; none
more necessary to be sol\ed, before Mixed
Cultivation becomes _as'common as Mr.
Russell would wish it to bte in Demerara,

ploring it from below where the bees were found

YU LU NIAL.
(Erom the Trin Chronicle.)
Great complaints of theft of growing provi
sions come from Demerara and Jamaica. Ev
erywhere in these islands there are fellows who
live no one knows how, too lazy to work, and
ever scheming how they can relieve thoir ne
cassities from tha toil of the industrious. Thae
Morning Journal thinks a little flogging would
be a good deterrent. Night patrols were ap
pointed in soms places.
Such is the secarcity of native provision
about Old Harbor that the shopkeepers there
are selling a quart of flour worth 2d. for the
pledge of a gnart of coff:e berries of the ensa
ing crop, worth at least 9d., an extortionate
bargain.
'L'he third shipment of Pine apples this sea
gon at Antigua for England was sent on board
the R. M. S. on the 20th ult.
Governor Longden has made a very long
and exhaustive Budget address, deseribed by
the Demerara Times as both disappointing and
discouraging, but by the Royal Gazette as
having on most points, met with publio approv
al. He abandons for tho present the project
to extend the Mahaica railway to Berbice (43
miles further), justifies the re-imposition of
the old duty on Flour and suggests a Govern
ment Printing Office. 'The fallacy of reason
ing on the first i 3 ably exposed by the Gazetie,
and the Times shews the great error of the
second. We see no remark on the third, which
would surely be as great a financial blunder in
Demerara as it has proved here,—where, on
the admission of the Government printer, the
paper used alone costs as much as was given
for the whole work wnder contract (most ridicu
lously uanderpaid, as now perfectly understood,
however), and where in the name of economy
a leap was made to thres or four times;the
previous outlay. Call it anything else you
please, but certainly not economy. With ma
ny fold more for rent, many fold for paper, a
bigger pay list (though under the contractor, a
newspaper was printed and other private work
done for a smaller weekly outlay), expensive
plant and an assured salacy, what is meant by
economy ? Better call things by their right
names,
Mr. Russell has given notice he will move
that premiums, not exceeding SIOO.OO in all,
be offered to any one who shall introduce into
the colony improved machinery for econgmi
cal tillage of the soil.
Experiments made at Windsor Castle for
tunately prove that it will not pay the planter
to degrade his sugar to suit American impors
ers. : .
A trade has lately sprung up in the Bahamas
in the export of cave earth or manure, found
in the numerous natural caves of those islands in
considerable quantity (a small export duty has
just been levied on the novel produect). In ex
amining the caves for their manure, a few
relics of the old Indians, former inhabitants of
the group, have come to light. * Another cu
riously carved relioc of the aboriginal inhabi
tants of the Babhamas has been nmtly found
en a ledge in one of the caves at Long Island.
This was discovered while a party was ex
ploring for guano at the north end of the island
near Alligator Bay. Tt is made of liknuih
vitee, having the usual head—half buman, half
tortoise—and the tail of an alligator. It has
four feet. On the uppe~ part, about the centre,
a carved crossin an oval frame appears, sup=:_
ported on eachside by two half crosses. The.
length of the relio is about 3 feet. It isin ex
cellent preservation and the carving is as sharp
as if it had been turned out of the makers®
hands yesterday.” : \
The Colonist sgain recommaends offering the
Demerara expirees Crown lands in lieu of retura
passage, quoting the gain to Trinidad by that
system. They caunot d» be'ter. It will con
tent and settle their coolies as nothing else
will. Mr. Lougden knows our experience per
fectly, and could set it at work in the sistee
colony. Wuen weses the Sub-Intendant’s re
port to Governor Irving on the 14 coolie settle
ments of Trinidad, laid before Council on the
Ist, we shall have more to say on the matter.
The system has succeeded here marvellously,
and there are always fresh applications.
The coolie immigrant popu.ation in Jamaica
was 12,455 in 1873. 5,580 were still under

well, and the whole were described as a most

arrived in 1860, had bought Brier's Pen for
£6OO, and has since purchased another ram
of land for £2OO
THE SPECTATOR ON A COUPLE
PAPERS IN ENGLISH REVIEWS.
Every one? should read the anonymous
paper in the Fortnightly on what we could
have done for France or Belgium, if only
to see all that can be said on the weakness
of this country for operations on the Coan
tineut, The writer believes that Great
Britain could accomplish nothing in the
field from want of numbers, forgetting, we
think, what a spear-head & British corps
d’armée would make to any Continental
amy, except perhaps the Germanj and
that thie only fortification we could hope
to defend effectually would be Antwerp,
and even there we should be beaten :
“ The French once wholly or nearly
crushed, Germany could take its own
line as to Belgium. Her Generals would
be too wise to break their men’s heads
against the works of Antwerp, occupied
by a hundred thousand soldiers at least,
nearly half of them Euglish. They would
simply occupy the whole Kingdom up to
within gunshot of the fortress; dispose the
chief part of the force thus used so as to
check any sudden issue fromit; and then
trust to the sureeffects of time, and live
oun the country.”
That is just what we should question.
Fifty thousand Englishmen in a fortress
like Antwerp, with the sea for a base,
would, if we read our history aright, com
pel either Germany or France to make an
effort for its capture, which, within twelve
months, would overstrain either State, and
enable every enemy it possesses, assisted
by English mouey, to bring their armies
into the field. That we ought to be able
to do much more than this is true, but this
is not nothing.
Blackwood has a remarkable paper head
¢d, * France and Germany,” and evident
ly written by a man who thoroughly
understands the military condition of
France, but is not unwilling to lend her
rulers a little help in diffusing the impres
sion that France ils powerless. His state
ment is that the immense military credits
taken since 1871 have been spent secretly
on military matérel,—cannon, rifles, and
fortifications ; that the regiments have
been systematically thinned to save
money ; that the territorial army exists
only on paper ; and that France has not,
outside Algeria, 200,000 soldiers on active
duty. She is, in fact, powetrless, or “at
best only a second-rate power. That this
statement is in the main true, we belieye;
but then it is also true that the replacing
of war matériel is far advanced, that the
cadres are exceedingly numerous, ard that
France, outside her army, must contain at
least 600,000 thoroughly drilled men,
every one of whom would be in the ranks
at a fortnight’s notice. The Germans, if
they invaded, with their wonderful mobi
lity, would probably reach Pui!, but
whether they would a second time compel
it to capitulate is a different watter.

the Government of Spain, but it does not
appear that any stipulations have been made
for the abolition of slavery in Cuba. Spain
surely owes this country something, even
without considering the substantial benefit we
confer upon her by joining with other powers
in recognising that she has a Government.
But as usual, it will be found that we have
given away everything and asked for nothing
in return, not even so much that Spain should
carry out a few of the solemn promises she
has made during the last thirty years, to get
rid of a system that stamps her as utterly un
worthy to enter into the family of civilised
pations. Germany, if common report is to be
believed, had some practical acquisition in
view when she took the lead in recognising
Spain. But before Germany could obtain
possession of Porto Rico, some explanations
would be necessary with America. ~ There is
such a thing as the Monroe doctrine, somewhat
wild and illogical, no doubt, but sufficiently
powerful in its influence to cause America to
take a practical interest in the Spanish posses
sions in the West Indies. If Spain bartered
away Porto Rico, the American President
might put his foot down on the transaction,
and although Germany would very much like
a colonial possession, yet if it involved a con
test with America, the game would not be
worth the candle.
The Peruvian Government is certainly pos
sessed of great powers of persuasion. No one
would have imagined, after the revelations
which have been made of late years respecting
the Macao coolie traffic, that the Chinese
Government would so soon have acted upon
a poliey of forget and forgive as to conclude a
treaty of commerce and navigation with Peru.
The world listens with a vague sense of awe
as ,China concedes to Peru ‘the privileges
of 'the most favoured nations.” But the real
object of the treaty and its real importance are
in the fact that **it permits the free departure
from Chinese ports of labourers for Peru, under
guarantee from the latter Power for humane
and proper treatment of the emigrants.” So
runs the telegram, but’ before discussing the
affair at length it will be necessary to ascer
tain the details of the treaty. It is evident
that Peru bas done a good stroke of business,
and obtained. a substantial concession. She
hasbeen more sucoessful than we have been
in dealing,with China, Poru can obtain what

MQW gr@tgd,,;o our oplonies, the gﬁ'eot of

~was_to stop emigration to British eolo
altogether for the last eight years. The
eruvians, with the chapter ‘of the Macao
coolie trade mot yet closed, have only to ask
ST. CROT X "AVIS.
and to get what they want. We are so very
virtnous that we suppose if we cut ‘off our
own nose other people would cut off theirs,
and'it 1§ rathér ‘a shock to find that our exam
ple, ‘which is always for the good of others,
and never for our own—is not appreciated.
Our own emigration was stopped, but the
Macao trade flourished all the more. It is
evident that Peru will never want for labour.
So long as she gets it fairly and treats it fair
ly, no one need complain. Our own colonies
have tried ‘to get it fairly from China, and
have failed. The Chinese have shared the
common suspicion of good intentions even
when supplemented by honest performance.
Our own colonies languish for want of labour
while Peru can immediately find a substitute
for Macao in the Chinese ports. There is one
reflection, however, to which this matter gives
rise and which is ‘eminently consoling to a
student of English literature. The treaty
lessens the distance (though of course only in
an abstract or moral point of view), which the
poet evidently thought was very considerable,
between the two countries, when he talked of
gurveying mankind from China to Peru. The
poet little thought that Pern was destined to
take rank among ¢ the most favoured nations”
in the regard of China.
In all the great sugar consuming countries,
except perhaps I'rance, there is seen during
the present year a considerable increase of
consumption. The anticipated result of the
abolition of the dutics in IXngland has not yet
been fully realised, but with a good fruit
season there can be no doubt that the year’s
figures will show an increase that will be the
best justification for that measure. In the
United States the consumption for the seven
months ending July was 397,486 tons, against
343,178 tons in 1873, showing an excess of
54,308 tons, or at the rate of very nearly
100,000 tons a year. In Germany for the
twelve months which ended in June the con
sumaption was 295,002 tons, against 260,779
ton in 1873, or an excess of 35,000 tons.
In Austria the progress of consumption is equal
ly great, and with one or two esceptions the
same remark applies to other countries. On
the other hand the production during the pre
gent year will not show much, if any, advance.
Cuba is still distracted with civil war, and dis
turbed by the prospect of that vast change
which must socner or later revolutionise the
sugar industry of the island. Brazil is extend
ing the cultivation of ¢offce rather than of sugar.
The production of Java is limited—that of
Egypt, having been artificially bolstered up by
the Viceroy, is now feeling the effects of His
Highness's want of money, the splendid ma
chinery which was sent out lying just as it ar
rived, and valuable principally as old metal.
Vacuum pans can be had a bargain in Egypt at
the present moment. In Louisiana the keen
antagonism, which has occasionaily broken out
into deeds of violence, between the white and
black races is not favourable to sugar cultiva
tion. The production of the Antilles, in gen
eral, is not likely to be greater this year than
it was in 1873. In Mauritius there is a deficit
of 11,000 tons, and Queensland and Natal can
hardly be said to count seriously at present in
augmenting the sugar production of the world.
If the Journal des Fabricants de Sucre can find
comfort in the above facts in estimating the
immediate future as the beetroot industry, they
are no less significant as throwing some light
upon the prospects as cane sugar. What those
prospects are, the readers of these remarks in
the West Indies will be best able to judge.
The greater saccharine richness of the cane,
as compared with beetroot, is probably admit
ted by all who are practically acquainted with
the two things. But an admissioa of this kind
by those who are interested in the cane indus
try is of no possible use, unless it leads to prac
tical action. The utmost skill and science
have been brought to bear upon the beetroot,
with a success patent to all the world And
if the cane is not only to hold its own, but to
show still further its superiority to all competi
tors, thereisno {ime to be lost in invokirg the
best chemical and mechanical skill to this end.
Mr. Bouverie, at the last meeting of the Colo
nial Company, stated the problem tersely and
well when hesaid that every exertion must be
used to obtain a greater quantity of juice
from the cane, and a better class of sugar [rom
the juice. An increase of ten per cent. in the
quantity of the juice extracted—that is, juice
capable of being made into sugar—would turn
many estates now working at a loss into pro
fitable properties once more. It may not be
amiss to refer to some proceedings which have
recently taken place in the Chamber of Agri
oultura. of Guadeloupe. Seeing the greater
attention which was given to the production of
sugar by the best chemical skill in Europe, it
was suggested that the colonial administration
should take the matter up in some practical
way by encouraging soientific inquiries as to
rendering still more perfect the process for ex
tracting the juice, and collecting knowledge
that would be useful in securing a prosperous
fature for the sugar industry in the co
lony. "It ‘was proposed’ thatthe Chamsber
of Agriculture should nominate a.commis
-8i%1.1.' to; cpnsider the question, especially ag the
industry was passing through a erisis. ?nq'og
the members was comforted to think tha .if the
cane sugar was suffering from low 'prices; the
effect upon the beet produce was still' more
disastrous. During the discussion it 'was pro
posed that a premium of 100,000 francs, paya
ble partly by the cultivators, partly by the
manufacturers, and partly by the colony, should
be offered to any one who would discover
a process which shall give 80 per cent. of
juice instead of the 60 per cent. which is now
obtained. The Chamber approved of this pro
posal being considered by a commission which
was then and there appointed.

Fried Corn Bread
In a Southern home, where old
fashioned plantation dishes are
still to be found, they serve a de
licious
FRIED CORN BREAD
Here are the ingredients:
2 cups sifted corn-meal
Va cup white flour
2 baking powder
1 teaspoon salt
1 egg
1 cup milk
And the method is: Mix and sift
the dry ingredients. Then stir in
the milk in which the egg has been
beaten. This should result in a
rather thick batter. Drop it, a
large tablespoon at a time, into
deep and hot fat and cook in the
fat until rich, golden brown.

Elementary Boys
Form Ball League
The elementary school boys
formed a basketball league on
Thursday November 27, with
games scheduled for Mondays and
Thursdays at 3:45 p. m. Boys from
the North End and Center schools
as well as the boys from Holy Re
deemer are eligible to play. Four
teams are in the league and the
captains have been chosen as fol
lows: Richard Ward, the Giants;
Allen Gerstel, the Redskins; Buzzy
Sauls, the Tigers; Noel Meriam,
the Packers.
The elementary school girls are
also forming a league and games
will start next week. Just as with
the boys, the girls from the North
End and Center schools and the
Holy Redeemer school are eligible
to play. The girls will start their
games on Tuesday at 3:45 p. m.
promptly.
Sunday Night Snack
Sunday night suppers, though
they need not be elaborate, should
be substantial. There is really
nothing better for this occasion
than hot ham sandwiches served
with olives, pickles or a tart salad,
Directions for preparing the sand
wiches are given below.
HOT HAM SANDWICHES
Place slices of minced ham be
tween thin slices of bread. Cut in
triangular pieces and hold together
with toothpicks. Beat 2 eggs
slightly and add to them % cup
cream or milk. Dip the sand
wiches into this mixture and fry
in hot fat (350° F.) until nicely
browned. Remove the toothpicks
and serve.
The three day Victory Loan
drive at the North End School last
week netted $285 in addition to the
regular weekly purchases made by
the children.

Jinrikisha, Famed Oriental Taxi, May
Soon Disappear From Shanghai Roads
Jinrikishas are on their way out
of the street scene in Shanghai.
In order to increase man power in
other fields, the city’s transporta
tion authorities have approved a
three-year plan which will com
pletely eliminate the familiar rik
sha as a public conveyance.
Shanghai, China’s most import
ant commercial center, was the
scene of a riksha graft investiga
tion in 1934. It was found that
riksha boys cleared only $3 a
month, paying nearly half their
daily earnings to the company
from which they rented rikshas.
None of the carriages was coolie
owned.
An individual coolie could work
only about 16 shifts a month, as
competition for the rikshas was
great and there were not enough
to supply the demand. This meant
enforced idleness for many. From
70,000 rikshas in Shanghai the
companies made large annual
profits. With the advent of street
cars, automobiles'and bicycles, rik
shas were the cause of frequent
traffic snarls.
Rev. Jonathan Goble, an Ameri
can Baptist missionary and former
United Statfes marine under Com
modore Perry, conceived the idea
of the man-drawn carriage, says
the National Geographic Society,
but there are two versions of how
it actually came to be built. Ac
cording to one story, the mission
ary wished to provide some means
to get his invalid wife outdoors.
He believed a modified baby car
riage would solve the problem, and
showed a picture of a child’s
vehicle in Godey’s Ladies Book to a
Japanese carpenter, asking him to
try to make a similar carriage.
The other, version is that the
missionary while talking to a high
Japanese official suggested the rik
sha as a solution to the unemploy
ment problem then faced by Japan.
Whichever the case, the first rik
sha was built in 1871; a two
wheeled carriage with shafts by
which the runner pulled it. The

first license for a riksha was ob
tained in Tokyo. The original
vehicle was improved later by a
Japanese who designed new
springs, a hood and wheel guards.
In Shanghai a riksha costs be
tween sl7 and S4O; those rented to
the public cost about S2O, and on
each a small tax is paid to the
municipality. Some privately
owned rikshas have been equipped
with American balloon tires, shin
ing chrome wheel spokes, and lac
quered bodies and have had artis
tic coats of arms painted on their
sides. Before the war an American
could ride in a public riksha for a
week on what it would cost him
in carfare to ride a day in the
United States.
At all hours of the day and night
in oriental cities, the cries of rik
sha boys can be heard —warning
others of ruts, shouting to clear the
way, or heaping abuse on other
coolies. Their summer clothing
seldom varies; large straw hats,
loose coats, cotton shorts, and
straw sandles. When it rains, they
don prickly straw raincoats or
cloaks made of oiled paper. In
winter they wear long trousers and
padded coats.
Riksha boys usually do not
marry as they cannot support a
family. They work diligently and
have great endurance, sometimes
running 30 or 40 miles a day at a
rapid pace. Constant exposure and
inadequate food shorten their lives
considerably.
The oriental go-cart was called
jinrikisha because the name liter
ally means “man-strength-vehicle.”
In Japan the slang expression is
jin-riki, but the more polite term
is kuruma, which means wheel. To
foreigners it is known simply as
riksha.
At one time in Japan there was
an unwritten law of the road which
compelled riksha boys to keep
their carriages in well-regulated
single file, with the oldest and
most honored passenger in the
lead.
Greenbelt Woinen
*
Tumble, 22-19
The Greenbelt women took a
tumble last week at the hands of
the North Washington Recreation
Club’s Basketball team, losing the
game 22-19. This was the second
game played by the local worrieh;
the first was won 35-32 against
the TeeCees.
Greenbelt led at the end of the
first quarter, 14 to 7, but was held
down in the second period to an
absolute zero by the substitution of
two taller guards by the North
Washington team. The third
quarter saw the NW’s jump in the
lead by two points and then add an
additional point in the fourth for a
three-point margin at the end of
the game. Marjorie Jost, fleet
footed forward of the N.W.’s led
the scoring with 12 points while
Eileen Mudd led with 8 for Green
belt. Isabel Butler added 5, Betty
Dickson 4 and Marion Benson 2 to
complete the scoring for Greenbelt.
Ora Donoghue of the locals was
out with the flu.
Doris Asher, Helena Knauer and
Patty Bell put up a very effective
zone defense and did a good job
holding the North Washington
squad to just the 3-point lead.

Psychology Group
Meets At U of M
The Military Division of the
American Psychological Associa
tion has completed its first post
war conference at the University of
Maryland. The conference was de
signed to correlate all contributions
to Methodology in Applied Psy
chology that have come out of re
search during the war. Dr. George
A. Kelly, acting head of the Uni
versity of Maryland psychology de
partment and member of the Navy •
Aviation Psychological Branch of
the Bureau of Medicine and Sur
gery, was chairman of the con
ference.
The sessions featured discussions
and reports on the findings from
the various specific fields of re
search carried on as a part of the
Navy and Army War Programs.
Prominent psychologists who led
discussions at the meetings in
cluded Comdr. William A. Hunt,
Chief Psychologist of the Neuro
psychiatric Branch of the Navy
and also secretary of Military Di
vision, Col. John A. Flanagan, head
of Army Aviation Psychology, Dr.
Rensis Likert, Chief of Division of
Program Surveys of the Depart
ment of Agriculture and Capt.
John G. Jenkins, chief of Aviation
Psychology in the Navy and head
of the University of Maryland Psy
chology Department.
Featured in the conference was
a speech by Capt. Jenkins on “New
Fields and New Responsibilities for
the Psychologist.” He told the
members that, “The war has given
the psychologists their greatest
chance to aid in benefitting man
kind. The future of Psychology
now lies with you.”
The members of the conference
included from
twenty-six leading Universities.

“Oil for the Lamps of China” ♦♦ By Alice Tisdale Hobart
Western Life, Love Clash With
Ancient Ways of the
Far East
’(This is a serialization of the Cosmopolitan production
now showing at Warner Brothers’ Earle Theaten)
WHAT HAS GONE BEFORE
After three years in Manchuria as a field man for an
oil company Stephen Chase, disappointed by his Amer
ican sweetheart, Lucy, marries Hester Wentworth whose
father had died on the trip to China.
They live in a lonely company hut in Manchuria.
Stephen’s duties keep him from home much of the time.
The company adopts his plan to sell cheap lamps.
Hester loses her baby at birth. They return to the
“States" tvhen Stephen gets his vaction. Stephen is no
tified at the home office that he is to have charge of a
division.
Ho, a venerable Chinese, becomes his adviser and aids
him in understanding the psychology and customs of his
countrymen. Hester’s offer to teach one of Ho’s family
to speak English precipitates a quarrel in Ho’s household.
Stephen and Ho on a journey to the interior find evi
dence of thefts by one of the native merchants. Members
of the man’s family promise to make restitution.
When floods threatened to overflow the banks protect
ing the oil in storage Stephen met the emergency by
ordering the water in, thus saving the company heavier
loss.
Stephen was transferred from the Green Valley to re
build rundown company branches. Revolutionists upset
the established order and take control, exacting heavy toll
of trades people.
NOW READ ON
“The son of Ho!” exclaimed the white man rising, recog
nizing the third son of Ho, the son with the facial contour
of an aristocrat, and the heavy nose and mouth of a coolie.
Stephen had been hearing of this General Ho among the
revolutionists, but he had never connected him with the
family of his friend.
“Thy father?” .he asked suddenly conscious of the ring
on his finger.
“I know nothing of him. I belong to the revolution.”
Stephen felt the foundations
cut from beneath him. The fa
vorite son of Ho speaking like
this. Jle divined that had
been a clash of wills, and that
this favorite son had defied Ho.
General Ho sat at Stephen’s
Side, as his father had so often
sat, his knees far apart, his
hands resting upon them.
“My affairs are many,” he
said. “It is my duty to see that
this city is rid of the obstacles
to progress.”
Stephen was at a loss, not
knowing what to reply. Had
Ho’s son come to make an ex
ample of him, send him from
the city?
Then General Ho took a card
case from an inside pocket of
his smart uniform. He arose
and. holding his card in his two
hands presented it to Stephen.
“There may be times,” he
said, “when it will be useful to
the friend of my father.”
As soon as he was gone, Bum
ham rushed in.
“Look,” he cried, “they’re pick
eting the British hongs! They’re
not going to be allowed to do
any business. A boycott until
they give up this business. Are
they going to do anything to us?
What did the general want?”
“The general called person
ally. He’s Ho’s son, don’t you
remember him?” said Stephen.
“I don’t know what they’re go
ing to do to us. I shouldn’t be
surprised if at least a partial
boycott was started against us.
None of the Chinese have come
to the office, have they?”
“No, not a stool in the Chi
nese office is filled.”
“Get the other men in, will
you, Burnham?” Stephen asked.
When they had come, he said
to them: “We must carry on as
if all the Chinese were at their
posts. We must not let the lead
ers think that we are paralyzed
because the Chinese staff is not
here. At all costs we must pre
serve the face of the Company
until the Company sees fit to
withdraw us.”
* * *
Grim Days
The October day with its as
tonishing happenings was draw
ing to its close when Stephen
thought it safe for them to go
out.
“It’s quiet in the city now,”
he said. They’ve cleared it of
the old regime. Let’s walk to
the edge of the settlement and
see what’s doing, Hester.”
They went along the deserted
streets until they reached the
gate of the concession where
they stood for a moment.
Stephen felt Hester clutching
his arm, her eyes on the flags,
and then he saw above them,
raised on poles, a row of bloody
heads.
"Dear, I shouldn’t have
brought you. I didn’t realize...
I thought the Young Revolution
itsts...” His sentence trailed
off.
Now he noticed right up tc<he
gates of the concession, there
TafapAona District 7000
were posters, bloody-looking
things.
Stephen thought uneasily of his
men at substations far off in
the interior, and of the man
and wife at the isolated fortress
of oil below the city. “I’ll tele
graph the men in the interior to
come in,” he said to himself.
“Down at the tanks they’re safe,
I think.”
“Hester,” he said suddenly,
“I wonder if I should have got
word to Riley. He’s alone
there working on the new tank.
Perhaps he won’t be prepared.
Anyway he’s living on the
launch. If nothing happens be
fore night, he’s got a good
getaway.”
They were about to turn
back when from a dark corner
beyond the gate a coolie
stepped forth and spat full in
the face of Hester.
“Oh-h-h.. .Give me your hand
kerchief, Ste ph e n.” As she
wiped her face, first with his
handkerchief, then with her
own, she turned hurriedly, al
most running down the street.
“Stop, Hester,” whispered Ste
phen. catching up with her.
“Don’t let them think we’re
afraid. The game is up then.
“Hester, I shouldn’t have
taken you out,” Stephen kept
saying with remorse, “but don’t
cry so. Why, sweet, you’re not
hurt.”
She was leaning against him
now, and as his arm encircled
her, he was conscious of the
slightness of her body.
“Dear, there isn’t any reason
for you to stay here. I want
you to go to Shanghai.”
Hester lifted her head, look
ing at him. “Really, do you
want me to go, Stephen?”
“No.”
“Stephen, I’m not going
away.”
“I shouldn’t have said I
don’t want you to go. I haven’t
any right to keep you. You’ll
be the only woman.”
“I know. Elsie isn’t coming
back to China until after the
baby comes.”
“Bumham says he wouldn’t
have her here, as things are,”
said Stephen. “He’s right. But
it’s different with us. We have
only each other.”
CHAPTER 34.
The next morning the revo
lutionists began gathering on
the waterfront and wharf—
coolies and ricksha men, water
carriers and, lowliest of all, the
carriers of night soil, gathered
around the revolutionists.
Those in the hong had little
to do but to watch the bright
spectacle. That dreaded evil, a
boycott, had fallen upon them.
Not the drastic brand used
against the British; only a par
tial boycott.
(To Be Continued)

text:
6 ’Oil for the Lamps of China” ♦ ♦ By Alice Tisdale Hobart
Western Life, Love Clash With
Ancient Ways of the
Far East
(This is a serialization of the Cosmopolitan produc
tion soon to be released by Warner Brothers.)
WHAT HAS HAPPENED
Stephen Chase, youthful and ambitious, is traveling
over the red plains of Manchuria. He is a salesman for
the gigantic American OH Company, opening new mar
kets in the primitive Far East. Stephen has been doing
this icork for two years, since he was graduated from an
American college of mines and, as a result of the mild
1908 business depression, had taken the China job.
He and his native servant, Kin, with their mule cart,
, are slowly slogging from town to town as Stephen in
dulges in habitual daydreams of his sweetheart, the lovely
' Lucy, in far-off America.
He calls on Merchant Yang to offer him the local oil
company agency. Yang declines and recommends Wen
Hon, who accepts. A feast celebrates the event.
Returning to the company office he tells Jim, the sec
ond in seniority, of an idea of selling cheap lamps to
1 coolies. Following a stag dinner the hilarious party
visits the Jap-town section.
Filled with remorse Stephen writes Lucy asking for
giveness. She replies expressing disappointment and
doubt. But she cables him to meet her in Japan in Au
gust. The company adopts his lamp idea.
Lucy, arriving at Yokohama, refuses to marry Stephen,
who meets Hester Wentworth, a tourist. He tells her of
his lost love and they marry a few days later.
NOW READ ON
The short-lived northern autumn gave Stephen and
Hester time only to skim the surface of their union. After
office hours, Stephen showed his wife the town, its few
straggling streets running almost immediately into the 350,-
000 square miles of the great Manchurian plain, the com
pany’s potential market, every city, every town known to
Stephen after long arduous travel.
He took her to the recreation
ground where the white inhabi
■ tants gathered for tennis. In
the matting shed, around the
tea-table, he introduced her to
the women. They were idly po
lite, secretly avid in their in
* spection of her.
It was only in the night when
she crept into his arms, that
Stephen dipped below the sur
face of his being, groping for
some way to rend asunder his
heart’s closure.
The month was gone and
once more there was a moon.
Out of the Manchurian night,
it hung high above the tide
washed town, transforming into
things of romance the mud
walls of compounds, the black
gates with their brass dragon
knockers, the refuse heaps, and
Tim’s rococo palace alike.
Sense of Bondage
Hester stood at the window,
gazing out at the hoar-frost
of salt left by the tide, which
spread its ghostly blight over
the garden. It was beautiful
in the moonlight. She reached
her hands out, locking her fin
gers in ecstasy. Wife of Stephen,
number three in one of the
hundreds of offices of the great
oil corporation, filled with the
mysterious significance of her
self!
"See, Stephen, I’ve put on
my wedding-nightgown.” Her
voice had a new quality, not
quite the voice of her maiden
hood. Still, though, a fragile
child in a child’s shift, he
thought folding her in his
arms. Never must he let any
thing harm Hester.
He felt his energies doubling
to work for her... And some
thing more... a faint sense of
bondage to his job. He needed
it in order to give Hester all
she should have. He knew she
wanted a piano. Could he man
age it? The freight alone...
j, And then his mother. He must
send her money this winter.
Exchange was against him. Chi
nese silver would buy so little
American gold.
In the morning, Kin superin
tended the coolie in stuffing
cotton into the cracks around
the windows. Then with his
delicate tapering fingers, he
pasted narrow strips of rice
paper along the cotton-stuffed
. cracks. That afternoon he
packed the master's winter
clothes for a trip, and the next
day Hester was left in the care
of the cook, the coolie and
Kin’s wife, appointed by Kin
to be her amah.
CHAPTER 8
Stephen was back in the
accustomed groove of travel.
He could not release his
thoughts from Hester. Hester
alone at night...their house
not so far from the king of
the robbers. Paid to protect
them, the robber chief, but
suppose he did not. Stephen
realized that he should have
taught Hester to use a revolver.
It was too soon anyway to
leave her alone. She didn't
know a half-dozen words of the
language. Why, even the men
Fine For Digestion,
ft
T.f.p/ion. Dlttrict 7000
*
were given a longer time to
get adjusted. Hie company was
asking too much.
Never before had he ques
tioned the discipline under
which he worked. No matter
how difficult things had been,
he had never turned back from
a trip. Whatever the condi
tions, he had met them in
silence, proud that he func
tioned so well for the company.
What before he had not
trusted himself to decide, he
now decided. Not all of these
inspection trips on which the
boss sent him were vitally
necessary, and this was one of
them. Three years of expe
rience had given him the knowl
edge, he believed, which justi
fied this conclusion. The boss
was too keen not to know this.
Why then had he sent him?
Testing him, perhaps, to see if
initiative, individual judgment,
had developed in him.
Ignored
Decision swept over Stephen
in a vitalizing wave. He com
manded the carter to turn
from the way, make the near
est telegraph station. He’d
wire the boss that he had col
lected the desired information
and was returning. As a mat
ter of fact, he had it all in his
notebook. A smile played about
Stephen’s mouth. The next
morning in the cold gray light
of 4 o’clock Stephen drank
hastily the coffee Kin brought
and then helped with the bed
ding roll and the packing of
the food-box in order to hasten
their start. He intended to
make the railway town by
evening. He'd be home by
midnight.
He had wriggled into his
place in the cart between the
grain sacks; the carter was
ready. Where was Kin? He
leaned forward peering around
the side of the cart. He could
just dlscefn lOn in conversa
tion with som™ one. Hang it
all! There was always so much
palaver to get through with
before starting.
“Come, come, hurry!” he
called.
“Lai, lai, coming, coming,”
answered Kin. “An electric
letter for the master.”
Stephen read the telegram in
the light of his flash. There
was but one word, “Proceed.”
Moodily he reversed his orders.
Never before had .he felt as
he did at that moment. When
the company ignored his part
in the lamps, there still had
been a certain personal satis
faction in the fact that his
handiwork had proved itself.
But this contempt for his
judgment was another thing:
this futile journey prostituted
his intelligence. Jim was right.
The little old pay check was
the only thing. That day the
way was long, life reduced to
an aimless performance of
duty.
(Follow This Thrilling Serial in
Tomorrow’s Paper)

THE RETIRING SYSTEM.
There is % Ke dera! statute which
empowers the President to retire, at his
discretion, any officer of the army who
has attained tiie age of sixty two years,
or who has served forty-five years as a
.commissioned officer. Under tLis stat
ute the President, a week ago, retired
Quartermaster-General Meigs, at his
own request. Meigs had -attained the
age of sixty-five years, hut as long as
he did not care to he placed on the re
tired list, with an annual salary of
91,123 for life, he was permitted to re- <
main in active service. As successor to
Quartermaster-General Meigs, the Pres
ident nominated Colonel D. H. Rocker,
who was Assistant-Quartermaster-Gen
eral under Meigs. The absurdity of
thi3 nomination becomes apparent w hen
it is known that Rucker is 70 years of
age, five years older than the mau he ’
was appointed to succeed, .end who was
retired on account of old age. Colonel
Rucker is General Sheridan's father-iu
law, and it ap;*ears that togeth
er they mustered sufficient influence
to get the President to make a nomma- \
tion which, if it were not that parti
sail-, particularly those who hold or
expect to get office, feel it tlieir duty
to defend every act of the Administra
tion, would bring Arthur into contempt
with every person. Republican or Dem
ocrat, who believed tnat the Govern
ment was framed for the people, not
for the party which for the time direct
ed its affairs. The law was intended to
be uniform in its operations, but left it
to the discretion of the President to
make exceptions in rare cases where
the public service required it. The
favoritism shown by the President in
the nomination of Colonel Rucker has
been approved by the /senate, and
Rucker is now Quartermaster General.
It w ill be in order for the President to
retire him at his own request in a few
mouths, as his promotion increases his
pay on the retired list fiom £3,375 to
£4,125 a year for life. Such favoritism
as this was displayed by Hayes on sev
eral occasions, bat especially in the
case- of Army paymasters, several of
whom were selected from young men
who had never served a day in the
Army, though there were thousands of
veterans who had fought in the war
who would gladly accept the rank and
pay of Majors in the regular Army to
which paymasters are entitled. Favor
itism is being carried to such extremes
in the distribution of civil arid military
-offices as to be disgusting, and sooner or
later the people will demand a change.
COOLIE IMMIGRATION.
The recent treaty with China has
given quite an impetus to coolie immi
gration. Chinese are now arriving in
^iau Francisco at an average rate of
about 1,500 a week. The British
steamer Suez arrived there a few days
ago with 773 coolies. A city ordinance
requires that all Chinese arriving from
foreign port# must be vaccinated, so
the six companies to whom the coolies
were consigned iiad the operation i*cr
fornted at the rate of 25 cents each.
Had not Hayes, who is now conceded
to be one of the greatest frauds of the
age, even by those w ho endeavored to
palliate his inexcusable acts, while oc
cupying a position to which lie was not
elected, vetoed tins bill to restrict Chi
nese immigration, the hundreds of
white immigrants who come to this
coast weekly by railroad would iiud it
less difficult to get employment at liv
ing wages, and the Chinese question,
which seems to be purposely kept un
settled by the politieiaus, would have
been eliminated from politics.
Central <Irant is rapidly losing in
fluence, owing to the new phase of
, character developed. The man who
was supposed to have an iron w ill and

tered is proving to be one of the most

A Star Route case opened at Wasbv.ig
ton w ith Fred K. Cleary. Postmaster of
Sydney, on the stand. He refused to
testify, lest he might criminate himself.
The prosecution spent the gr ater part
of the forenoon in arguing to compel
Cleary to testify. The Court overruled
the attempt, 03 CTtary was odc of the
conspirators indicted. The testimony
of persons who traveled the Sydney and
Dead wood route at the time covered by
the indictments, and also that of the
Dead wood Postmaster, was tuken. It
is thought the case w ill go to t..e jury
on Saturday.
Tt.ere is an almost unanimous desire
in toe army to have a bill passed re
tiring officers from active service who
have attained the age of sixty-two.
This feeling is not confined to tile
younger men in the ranks, w ho will
benefit by the law, but is entertain ed
by the older, and a petition recently
forwarded to Washington, urging tbe
passage of the bill, contained one thou-'
sand four hundred names of military
men, many of them being men who

Peter Sullivan, one of the men impli

Tne recent great storm in Texas is

Central at Chamber's Creek, and the

Mr. Lloyd, temporary magistrate of

Tombstone is excited over a rumor

As near as can be ascertained there

The nomination of Joliu C. New, of
Indiana, to be Assistant .Secretary of

It is said that New could have had a

lUiurn Jennings, a uoteil polyga

Justice Stephen J. Field, of the

A portion of the land grant of the

Scoville is busy preparing a bill of

The circulation of standard silver dol

The diptheria at Cleveland, Ohio,

Thomas R. Lagan, known as “Dick

At Chicago there have been seveu

Carrie liradley’s house at Portlaud,

velopments calculated to throw any

accused were rm.de.
The steamer Bahama, from P> rt©

6 THE DAILY EVENING TELEGRAPH PHILADELPHIA, WEDNESDAY, SEPTEMBER 28, 1870.
JUDGE HELLO'S VIEWS.
A Letter t the VorUlna;mii.
The following letter from the Hon. W. D. Kclloy,
Irlvlng Tile views upon the coolie labor question, will
)t read with interest : .
TO TUB WORKWOMEN OF miI.ADEI.rHI A.
ftetitleinen: Justice to you, In common with the
laboring people of the country at large, requires
we to invite your attention to the following state
ment: I regret the necessity that compels me to make
tLis communication, for 1 hare no taste for per
sonal controversy, and never engage in It willingly.
But it cannot always be avoided. Defamation may
fee pressed to a point at which, endurance ceases to
be a virtue and becomes a shame, as silence may
fee construed by the community Into admission of
the truth of falsehoods widely and persistently cir
culated by one who has hitherto enjoyed the confi
dence of the community. Yet, notwithstanding
this, were the criminal practices to which I am
&bout to refer intended only to affect my personal
nd political character, I would, in the faith that
mj daily life would amply refute them, treat them
with the silent contempt with which I have hitherto
treated such aspersions; but these falsehoods are de
signed and Intended to afiect your rights and inter
ests, as well as my character, and my duty to you
constrains me to expose them, loathsome as the
task may be.
In May last, while the tariff' bill was before Con
cress, the leading free traders of the country as
sembled in convention at Washington for the pur
pose of devising means by whicti the protective
provisions of the existing tariff should be repealed.
They found that though there were a few mis
guided Republicans in the House who favored free
trade, that the body of the party, constituting a
majority of the House, were in favor of sucli pro
tection as would at least secure to the laborers of
America our home market at the existing rates of
wages. Having, therefore.no hope that they could
induce the present Congress to establish tree trade,
they determined to try to elect a majority of anti
rrotectionists to the next Congress. Their conclu
sions were published to the country in formal edi
torial letters to their leading organs and in a brief
nummary through the Associated Press, ami will
doubtless be remembered by many of you.
Jn order to secure their object, they agreed to
Consider the question of free trade or protection as
the only issue in this Congressional contest, and
that if the Republicans in close districts nominated
j rotectionists, they would vote for the free-trade
candidate, though he were a Democrat, and that in
districts which are decidedly Republican and pro
tective, in which a free-trade Republican could be
induced to run as an Independent candidate, they
would, in compensation for this, exert their influence
to induce the Democrats to nominate no candidate,
and support the renegade Republican. In pursuance
of this programme, which was, as 1 have said, an
nounced to the country in May last, and which, if
successful, will in two years close half the work
shops and mines, and reduce the wages of every
workingman in the country, William li. Thomas is
an independent candidate for Congress in the
Fourth district, and the Democratic convention
Las nominated no candidate; and should ho bo
eiectrd, let his personal wishes be what they may,
le ilier have to unite with those who hive
ji,'i::...t ; for your destruction, or cheat and da
those who manipulated the Democratic con
tention, and thus secured him the remote possi
bility of election.
The importers and agents of foreign manufac
turers, who furnish the fuuds with which the war
against our tariff is maintained, know mo to be a
pronounced and extreme protectionist. They know
that as soon as the war closed I announced my pro
gramme for the future, and that it was most dis
tasteful to them, as It involved. the postponement
of the payment of the public debt, and the earliest
possible repeal of internal taxes, and the removal
of duties from all articles of food or raw materials
lor manufactures, such as tea, collec, spices, ivory,
chalk, paper stock, sulphur, argols, kryolite, and
an infinite number of tropical productions.
1 say that they know that such were the views I
entertain, and am constantly pressing upon the ear of
the country, for the war had scarcely closed before
1 proclaimed them iu a speech, of which more than
fcalf a million copies have been circulated in
pamphlet form. It was made in the House of
.Representatives, January 31, 180iJ, in favor of pro
tection to American labor, and I submit the follow
ing extract as an illustration of its spirit:
"1 am not anxious to reduce the total of our debt
and would, in this respect, follow the example of
England, and as its amount has been fixed would
not for the present trouble myself about its aggre
gate except to prevent its increase. My anxiety is
the tuxes it invoices shall be. as little oppressive
as possible, and be so adjusted that, while d tending
ear industry against foreign assault, they may add
nothing to the 'cost of those necessaries of life which
ive camud produce, and for which we must therefore
Ivck to other lands. The raw materials entering into
cur manufactures, which are yet unable to pro
duce, but on which we unwisely impose duties, I
would put into the free list with tea, coffee, and
ether such purely foreign essentials of life, and
would impose duties on commodities that compete
with American productions, so as to protect every
lecble or infant branch of industry, and quicken
those that are robust. would thus cheapen the ele,
mi nts of lij'e, and enable those whose, capital is eia
tnirktd in any branch of production to offer such vjfies
to Die skilled workman oj all lands as would steadily
tuui rapidly increase our numbers, and, as Is always
the case in the neighborhood of growing cities or
towns Of considerable extent, increase the return for
farni h'bor; this policy would open new mines and
quarries, build new furnaces, forges and factories,
and rapidly Increase the taxable property and taxa
ble inhabitants of the country."
In view of these circumstances, and tha fact that
the Fourth district of Pennsylvania is the greatest
manufacturing district iu the country, and that a
majority of its voters depend upon their labor for
their life, the undertaking of Mr. Thomas would
seem to be a desperate one. To succeed ho must
Lave the votes ot the workingnieu of the district.
With these against him his canvass would be hope
less from the start. How were these to be secured'.'
There was nothing in his personal history to arouse
the sympathy of workingnieu; tor, although lie is
abundantly rich, and has for more than thirty
years been a large purchaser of labor, he has at no
time, in auy ot their struggles, ottered them a
helping band or words of sympathy. It would not
do to proclaim the glittering fallacies of the freo-
trade school, ana euueavor 10 pemuaue uie intelli
gent workmen of the district that our government
is bound to allow importers to buy the productions
of labor where thev can buy them cheapest, and
pay but nominal duties upon them. No! His con
victions upon this subject, and his entanglement
with the Free Trade League must be concealed,
and there was therefore nothing which promised a
chance of success but the destruction of the couii
dence of the workingnieu of the district in his com
petitor, the present representative.
A more conscientious, a less reckless man than he.
before undertaking this work, would have inquired
whether facts existed by which it might probably
le accomplished. Hut to Win, li. Thomas, as
events have proven, this was a question of small
moment. With the reckless disregard of truth of
which 1 am about to convict him, he evidently
concluded that he could make all the facts he
needed, and that you do not remember the local
history of the past ten years, and are unable to de
tect a falsehood though it relates to your own inte
rests and the proofs are all before you. Reference
to the nauseous personal slanders with which he
garnishes his public speeches would be out of place
ere; nor will 1 pause to notice the rancorous but
ludicrously absurd falsehoods with which he has
crowded his autobiography, recanUy published,
with a tattering likeness of the author, un
der the title ot resolutions and speeches of
t.tiural Wm. B.Thomas and FJward D. Stokes,
Ksq., but may remark that having been printed for
. gratuitous distribution, it can be had at the street
corners, and may be accepted as a curiosity. My
purpone at this time is to bring to your attention
the falsehoods and forgeries perpetrated by Mr.
Thomas in his letter of September 1 to Science
Council of United American Mechanics.
This mendacious epistle appeared at length iu
most of the city papers ot the 2d lust. It purports
to have been written iu reply to a request fjr an ex
pression of his opinions ou the coolie question, but
iu almost wholly devoted to the falsification of my
Opinions ou the subject. To this end Mr. Thomas
cites a portion of a speech he had made as early as
the 18th of July last. It is as follows :
"However devoted Judge Kelley and his chosen
organs may be to the doctrine of a protective Unit,
they are in favor of free trade in at least one a tide
io which I am most decidedly opposed, f allude to
the traffic in coolie Chinese laborers. lvKlleY him
self is enthusiastically in favor of the introduction
jf this Bpecies of labor. In order to obtain the pub
lication of his views on this subject, the J ndge mm
aged to get himself interview ed by a reporter of his
organ, The Press, who reports his sayings at great
length in that paper of Augusts, Ki'ii."
Why was my name intioduced into a letter, the
Kile object ot which was to express the opiui u of
the wriu-r ou a direct question as to Ir.s ou opin
ions? Was it not iu the hope of securing his elec
tion to Congress by impressing the geiitU-iueu t
whom he wrote with the conviction that 1 was fal-n
to their interests and the pledges of mv Pt life".'
Hut you may say that the introduction of 111 y nam )
is a mere mutter of tante, and that two real q'ie
tion is, "is Mr. Thomas' statement irue?" And to
this question I answer, "No, and ha knew it to
be ftt.se when he wore it." Tho man docs not live
who Lus btfurd ljO bay on word in favor of tha
traffic In coolie Chinese labor." 1 have always
been opposed to It, and in 1802 assisted in framing
and passing a law to prohibit, if possible, its intro
duction into this country; and 1 again assert, and
will proceed to prove, that when William H.
Thomas penned that paragraph bo knew that he
was writing a libellous falsehood, in support of
which he could not produce a particle of honest
proof.
He had written to General John A. Logan and
other gentlemen with whom I had differed in the
debate on the tariff, begging thorn to send him
some evidence that I was in favor of coolie labor,
and I dare him to publish their replies. But more
than this, he had before him when he wrote that
letter, and professes to quote from it, my inter
view with Mr. Gilbert as it appeared in The Press
of Augusts!?. 18G9. not 1870. as he says. As he had
preserved it for aearly a year, and found it to be
his only resource, he had doubtless conned It well
enough to discover that it related to what 1 had
seen of the voluntary emigrants from China then
on the Pacific coast, and not to the question of the
importation or einplovment of coolies, and that its
whole drift was decidedly against it. What do you
think of the honesty of the man who, to induce vou
to vote for him that he may betray your interests,
iblishes the assertion that I am "enthusiastically
in favor of the importation of Chinese coolie la
bor," and attempts to prove it by a garbled extract
from a paper which he knew to embrace the fol
lowing passage:
"Question Is our commerce with China likely
to increase with much rapidity; and if so, what
will be the probable effect of its increase upon the
industries of the country?"
"Answer So much for the answer to
the lirst portion of your question. With regard to
the remaining portion, concerning the ultimato
effect of this rapid increase of our new commercial
relations upon the country at large, the answer IB
far more difficult. To state that answer satisfac
torily would necessitate a full interpretation of
the luturo policy ot tuo people 01 this country witn
regard to the Chinese laborer; and of course any
statement ot that Kino at tnis ttme would necessa
rilv be based upon the indefinite probabilities of
the future. This much, however, I may say: If we
are true to our Republicanism and cnrtstianity tne
effect must prove highly beneficial to both coun
tries. Hut if our people adopt the policy of selfish-
nets, and regard the Chinese as J ree-traders regard
all ifrkingmenas mere raw Material, to be con
sumed in the cheap production of goods, no one
can predict the consequences. Thus far tho
latter policy has prevailed, and you will
find China existing in San Francisco and
Sacramento as palpably as in Canton or Hong
Kong. In San Francisco the Chinese quarter is a
distinct and separate locality. Passing through
certain squares of Sacramento street, you are in the
Chinese market. Look into what store you will,
and the parties encased in it are Chinamen and the
customers Chinese. You visit either of two the
atres at night, and you find the actors are Chinese,
the audience or tne same nationality , and rue pi
a portion of the history of China, continued from
the last evening, and to be continued on the next,
and so on through the year. They have their gamb
ling saloons, and their resorts for the victims of
opium, witn tew exceptions, tne women 01 tneir
race are brought over as matters of commerce.
They are women from tho rivers, and not from the
interior ot cnina. xnuB tne uninese trom our wes
tern shores, as a class, retain their individuality
and their national characteristics; theu are by force
of circumstances a distinct and separate people; and if
as such they are to pcrvaac ine country, tuetr presence
among us will be a constant source of annoyance,
uisturoance anauangcr."
If to denounce a thing as a constant source of an
noyance, disturbance and danger, and as fraught
with consequences which no one can predict, is to
advocate it, then Wm. li. Thomas is a truthful and
honest roan, and has not attempted to swindle the
workingnieu of the Fourth district into voting with
the Free-Trade League. If he has the proof that I
have uttered one word in favor of the employment
of coolie or other laborers at wages lowor than the
average American rates, why does he not produce
it ? Having made tho assertion, why does he at
tempt to sustain it by falsehood and forgery ? If
there be proof he has it, for he has employed my
bitterest foes to trace the record of mv life step bv
step for the last thirty years, as is shown by refer
ence to events of that duto in his autobiography. If
thev have found proof on this point, let him pro
duce it and show honest men that they may with
safety rely upon his word.
"What!" I think I hear some of you exclaim,
"was Wm. B. Thomas lying when he told us what
vou said through ThelPress of the 10th and 23d of
July, 1G9Y" Yes, gentleman, Mr. Thomas cut that
entire story trom tho whole ciotn. 1 never uttered
or wrote one sentence of what, in his infamous let
ter to Science Council, he says 1 published In The

of either of these dates, (for I have examined them,

There is not even a shadowy foundation for' the

on the days referred to by iur. xnomas 1 was not in
tho United States, or within telegraphic reach of
them, but was on board the steamer Oriflamme.
traversing the Pacific Ocean. Yet, when speaking
of me in this infamous letter, so deliberately pre
pared, and to circulate which he has expended so
much money, he says:
"He also speaks through his principal organ, The
Press, on July 10, 18G9, as follows: "
"Able-bodied Chinamen work in China for about
two dollars a month, and are glad to get that. It is
but fair to suppose that home labor is cheaper. Un
der the contracts by which they are brought from
China they are to work out their passage money at
four dollars a month. Allowing the contractor fifty
per cent, more than the employer, he can afford to
supply us with the best of first-class labor at six
dollars a month. This labor, it must bo remem
bered, is trained, docile, respectful, and in abun
dant supply. To this matter we
earnestly call the attention of intelligence offices
and labor agencies of this city. They have in their
power to revolutionize their own business and the
social life of the place, and the first man who breaks
the ground will reap the harvest."
And aeain. Julv 23:
"By the termslof the Chicago platform the poli
tical creed on which Grant was elected, and which
he stands bound under his own baud to execute
our doors are open to the oppressed of all people
Surelv the Mongol is one of them.
"Koopmanschoop has left for California, but will
be back again in October to break ground in New
York for the introduction of this most valuable and
richly productive labor. We trust that he will not
be allowed to depart without giving us an experi
mental, quota, at least, of his first cargo. Let us
not be behind New York In obtaining this new
labor, which is simply condensed weaitn. our
mines, our public works, our private grounds, our

from her teeming plains, burdened with the surplus

Was there ever perpetrated a viler and more fla

01 tne recKtess audacity or Air. Thomas, some ot
you may ask whether he has mado no honest quo

nearest approach to ft is in the following Instance,

Yesterday afternoon, while some little
girls were bathing at a part of the shore in
Water Gut known as Lawyer’s Bay, oune
of their number, named Catherine Bryan,
got out of her depth, apparently, and was
drowned. We have not heard any reliable
particulars of this unfortunate accident,
but the place may be dangerous owing to
deep channels cut out by the “‘gats” which
there run into the sea.

TELEGRAMS.
ST. CROIX STATION.
New York, Juve 30th.—The Duke of
Marlborough, Lefore the Mayor of New
York, and subsequently before a Baptist
minister, was yesterday married to the
widow Mrs. Hamersley, noted for her
beauty and wealth.
The French Senate has censured the
Miuister of Justice, M. Ferouillat, for hav
ing dismissed the Substitute Procureur of
Carcassoune.
It is understood the Minister will resign.
No trustworthy statement can be obtaiu
ed respecting the Panama Lottery Loan,
which it is geuerally thought probable
will prove a failure. M. de Lesseps has
convoked a meeting of the Board of Direc
tors. The shares of the Company closed
higher. The Standard, in an editorial on the
subject, affirms that eveun the alleged
taking of 1,300,000 bonds will prove a
failure, as thav will give the Company
only 19,000,000 sterling, which will
melt away like snow under the expen
ses, the anunual interest alone being
4,000,000. The article further asserts that &
year hence the Company will owe 90,000,000,
including the capital without any assets.
London news states that the Jockey
Wood, who was aceused of pulling horses,
has recovered one tarthing damages in the
case against the Licensed Victuallers
Gazette.
Mr. Morley, in a speech at Chelsea, said
‘hat there was time yet to win the Irish
battle.
The recent debate had disclosed the fail
ure of coercion to suppress the League, and
there was no crime low except such as
resulted from coercion.
Martinique, 30th June.—The English
barque “Bertha Aunderson”, with general
cargo from New York, part discharged,
caught five at 3:30 a. m. Fire originated
in main hateh, and having reached 300
cases Kerosine oil all hopes of saving ves
sel were given up, and she was towed out
of harbour aund abandoned. Cuause of five
unknown,
London, June 30th.—The two cables
between Java and Port Darwin intervapted,
catting off telegraphic communication with
Australia and New Zealand.
Lon‘ton, Jualy 2ud.—The Baron de Worms
is visiting the Freneh Ministers in Paris on
the question of the =g Convention.
New York, July 2nd.—Loadon news
states that Her Mijesty the Qween has
oiven the balance of the Women's Jubilee
offering, amounting to £70,000, to the St.
Cathering’s Training Hospital for Nurses
to the Poor.
Mr. Gladstone has written to the Edin
burgh Printers expressin his sympathy
in their efforts to sceure m wlifications in
the proposed American Copyvight Bill.
The Lrish Bishops have alopted resolu
tions deploring the Tand Laws, and in
which they ask for immediate Parliamen
tary action to prevent oppressive exactions
and arbitrary evictions, otherwise they
soar that grave disaster will follow.
It 18 announced that the Conservative
candidate has been elected for Thanet by
a majority of 633. Many consider the
result. a Liberal victory, comparing the
present Majority with the previous Conser
vative Majority of 2,008.
Paris despatches state that during last
week Panama Cannl shares fell sixty francs,
which is attributed to the bear movements
of the Credit Mobilier, which is trying to
float its own loans of three milliards.
M. de Lesseps has issued 2a circular
affirming that 800,000 bonds have been
taken, and that a syndicate will guarantee
the remainder.
It is feared that a ministerial crisis is
probable, as the new Budges Committee
means a grave check on the Cabinet, it
being composed of tweaty Opportunists,
seven Radiecals, and four Extremists.
The reported resignation of M. Ferouillat,
the Minister of Justice, is denied.
It is reported that Anarchists are plot
ting against the lives of the Kaiser and
Prince Bismarek.
London, July 3rd.—The Baron de Worms,
after an interview with M. Goblet, pro
ceeded to Belgium, No difficulties are
expected.
The libel suit brought by Mr. O’Donuell
against the Times has begun. The plain
tiff has employed only a siugle junior coun
gel, while the defendant has the Attorney
General, Siv Henry James, and a full bar.
The Times hopes to compel disclosures
respecting the secret history of the Land
League.
New York, July 3rd.—lt is noticed that
the Kaiser refrained from auy allusiou to
Eugland in his recent speeches, and
rumours are circulating that His Majesty
proposes to insist on Queen Victoria
restoring the dinry and other private
papers of the late Emperor, which disclose
family quarrels.
The North German Gazette publishes an
official report of the violation of the fron
tier by two French officers for the purpose
of talking sedition with the Alsatians. The
Gazette urges the Government to make
the Vosges the frontier line of Germany.
Paris despatches state that the second
ballots for the representation of Charente
have resul‘ed in the election of M. Sagial,
the Bonapartist.
M. Rouvier, on accepting the Chairman
ship of the Budget Committee, said that the
Committee had no political character, its
sole task being to reduce expenditure.
It is stated that the Attorney General of
New York has decided to prosecute the
Sugar Trusts for illegal combination.
DEMERARA NEWS.
( From The Argosy.)
ESSEQUEBO.
The planters have been praying for dry
weather for the last two mouths and uvow
that their prayers are answered they are
drawing very long faces. Their fear is
that the dry season will set in without
the usual tapering off of the wet one. A
great deal of cane planting is being done
here just now, 80 it is very desirable that
we should get some heavy showers to
make the plants catch.
The extension of cane cultivation on the
lower end of the Coast has afforded am
ple employment at fair wages for every
one who is willing to work. Tradesmen
have still got a little difficulty n getting
work, but there will be no cause for them
to complain after the end of this mouth,
which is the end of the crop year on most
of the estates in this district. Iu these
times, when the strictest economy bhas to
be practised to make the two ends
weet, Managers generally postpone re
pairs to machinery, houses, &c., from
the end of the erop till the beginning ot
the next. I know «f more than one es
tate where orders have been given ftor
the employment of considerable numbers
of tradesmen after the 30th June. Be
sides the work afforded by ordinary re
pairs, the erection of a new chimney and
new machinery at Reliance will tend to the
prosperity of this deserving class of meun.
—From the Trinidad papers we learn
that a deliberate attempt was made by »
Barbadian Servant named Miriam Moore,
to poison Bishop Rawle and his family,
on the 26th of May. The Gazette’s details
are as follows:—*On the morning of that
day, soon after respectively partaking of
beef-tea, tea and coffee, Bishop Rawle,
Mr. Moor and Mrs. Smith, and the cook
and two children were taken ill with vomi
ting. Dr. Pasley was immediately sent
for and promptly attended, and was
able to give relief to and rescue his pa
tients, after they had undergone con
siderable suftering. His Lordship the
Bishop was, from his age and feeble
health, so prostrated by the violence of
the vomiting that he was for a time in a
critical state. All the sufferers have now
quite recovered. The poison employed
has been verified by Mr. McCarthy, the
Government Chemist, to be arsenic, and
had, it would seem, been put into the kettle
in which the water was boiled with which
the beef-tea was made for Bishop Rawle,
and the tea and coffee of which the other
victims partook.”
King Ja-Ja is safely located in St. Vin
cent and receiving visitors. A gentleman
whoecalled Ly way of courtesy says heé found
the King was dressed, ready to go to a
lawn-tennis party at the Administrator’s,
where he had been invited. ¢“Hisdress for
the occasion was a black frock-coat and
trousers, with a waistcoat richly embroider
ed in gold, black silk hat and white kid
gloves. He spoke English but rather
broken and not with the fluency of his
son. He was very affable and bore an
appearance of command, sometimes of
dignity, that made it perceptible that
he had been a King in his country. . At
the lawun-tennis party, the King sat to
the right of the Administrator and the
Commauder of the Canada to the left.
The exiles appeared to enjoy themselves
greatly.
Coolie Disturbances,
The agitation amongst the coolie labourers
which manifested itself on the estates of
Goedverwagting, Turkeyen and Nonpareil,
last week, and in each case was suppressed
by the police without the slightest occa
sion to resort to forcible measnres, ap
peared this week on Hnmore and subse-
