An ebboxeocs impression appears to have
obtained with the public as to the nature of tbe
existing Terbal understanding between the Haw
aiian Government and the Pacific Mail Company.
Tbe appropriation made by tbe last Legislature
as a subsidy far ocean steam service, was $18,000
per annum for two years. This appropriation
was placed at the disposition of the Ministry;
but so far, no contract has been made with the
Pacific Mail Company. Up to the present time
they hate only been paid at the rate of 12.000
per annum, as we liate said, there is no sub
sisting contract whereby tbe Company's boats
are required to remain in this port any specified
time, or indeed, to touch here at all. And on
the other Land and for the same reason, the gov
ernment may, in its discretion, pay the subsidy
to any other line which shall perform the requi
site service.
Tue letter of tho Hon. Mr. Gibson, which
appears in another part of to-day's paper, con
cludes with some stirring and eminently just
remarks upon a suljcct to the consideration of
which this journal, as well as Mr. Gibson him
self, has frequently and earnestly directed the
attention of our government and people the
equalization of the sexes. We trust that Lis,
and other able pens in our community, will con
tinue the agitation of this matter of so vital
interest to the future welfare of the country,
until the requisite public opinion is aroused to
ensure decided action. At present, it would ap
pear that the most promising field to which we
may look for a supply of females to equalize the
proportion of sexes, is in tbo South Sea Islands,
where it will be remembered that the Slorrribird
on ber last voyage, procured more female than
male immigrants. And we are informed that
they hate ccnerally given Fatisfaction to those
who have employed them as servants.
In this connection we may remark, that while
the prejudices of caste will prevent the East
Indian Coolie from amalgamating with any other
race, no such difficulty is found with the Chinese
or the Portuguese immigrant. Wherever the
Portuguese Lave gone as colonibts, they have in
variably " increased and multiplied."
As to the immigration of a number of the
frcedmen of the South with their families, to
meet tbe pressing demand for labor here, we
certainly would bo glad to see them come, if they
can be got without difficulty, and without cre
ating any ill feeling among Southern agricul
turists. But we may add that we have recently
seen in print the statement that in some of the
Southern States it was proposed to legislate for
the purpose of discouraging attempts to procure
the emigration of tho frcedmen.
Dcrinc sreexsMTE jears, from 1870 to 1876,
this jvmrnal repeatedly and earnestly urged the
absolute nccessitj of protecting the forest and
undergrowth of Nuuanu valley from cattle, if it
was desired to preserve tLa a-at -ewppljr. - fiat
those in authority at that time did not believe in
the theory that the presence or absence of trees
or vegetation had anything to do with rainfall,
(therein differing from about all ti e rcet of the
world) and our appeals in favor of preserving
the forests were cither disregarded entirely or
ridiculed as quite visionary. At length, bow
ever, in 187C, a strong public sentiment Laving
been aroused on the subject, the government of
the day felt impelled to move in the matter, and
the cow pasture" at the -upper part of tbe
valley came into the possession of the Interior
Department, and ever since the pasturing of
horses and cattle there has been strictly tabued,
The result has been eurrrieinir, even to tbe
strongest a ' vocatcs of the measure. The whole
of that portion of the head " of the valley
which was formerly a dry and almost barren
waste, is now covered with a thick undergrowth
of grass and shrubbery, and young trees are
here and there making their appearance. All
the lower portions of the ground where formerly
no water could bo seen, are now knee-deep
swamps and bogs, forming altogether a great
natural reservoir, wherein is stored tbe moisture
distilled from tbe trade-wind clouds. This, as
the ground becomes surcharged, percolates down
and into the valley 6treairs and springs, and con
ctitutes a standing guarantee that the supply for
tbe city shall never again be curtailed as in
former years, when the " cow pasture ' existed
We trust that the lesson thus practically illus
trated in Nuuana valley will not be disregarded
by the government and by private parties who
may have control ol forest lands, or of any
lands wherein water courses take their rise. Tbe
apathy and in some instances, perhaps, tbe
ignorance exhibited for years in these Islands
in the continued destruction of the forests, will,
if persisted in, result in cur ruin as an agricul
tural country. And without agriculture, what
will these Islands be worth ? Anyone who lived
here twenty-five or thirty years ago must have
remarked the great change that has taken place
in many localities, where formerly running
streams were plentiful and deep, but where now
they either creep along as tiny rivulets or have
dried up altogether. This is a direct result of
;the de past urine of cattle and sheep, and tbe
greedy cutting of trees for firewood. While the
wood-cutter deprives tbo soil of the shade of
large trees, and lets in the 6un's rays whereby
the stores of moisture are evaporated, the cattle
and sheep tread down the undergrowth and de
stroy the young trees, which if left to grow
would take the place of the old ones. Almost
everywhere, all over the Islands, this work of
destruction and ruin is going on unchecked. We
may remark, however, that on quite a number of
sugar plantations the plan has been adopted of
late of using coal for fuel In the mills, instead of
wood. We should be glad to bo convinced that
the substitution of coal for wood by our planters
invariably resulted from a conviction of the
necessity of preserving the forests, rather than
from motives of economy.
The conservation of forests as a measure of
governmental policy and regulation, has long
since been the subject of legal enactments in
Europe, and more particularly in Germany,
where the forcetier " or Guardian of the
Forests is a regularly salaried officer, and where
tbe business of planting new forests to take the
place or the old ones is vigorously and constantly
carried on. Scientific men of Europe have clear
ly demonstrated tbe disastrous results to any
country wnicn follow tbe destruction of its for
ests. Of late years, a good deal has been written
on this subject in the United States, and public
opinion has been awakened to its importance.
Un tne lourth page of to-day's paper will be
found a forcibly written article from the New
York Nation, ebowing the desolation and ruin
towards which California is surely drifting if she
continues to recklessly destroy the forests of ber
foot-bills. May our own government and people
take warning in time.

Equalization of the Sexes.
Ma. Ennoa.- Permit me through your columns to ssy
a few words in regard to an Impression of certain diffi
culties In th way of our immigration of colored laborers
from the Southern States.
There is a plan on foot and now In operation to emi
grate a large body of freedmen into new, unsettled North
western Territory; and let me call your attention to a
dispatch In the San Francisco weekly Chronicle of 27th
February last, in support of my statement "A Washing
ton correspondent says: Four or five hundred.negrocs
from South Csrolina are here, and will start for Idaho in
three weeka to locate ou lands and work on the railroad
from Boise." Senator Bruce and other Congressmen aid
ing the emigration.
Tbla would appear to be a movement hoe tile to tbe in
terests of employer in the Southern States; but during
my recent visit to that country I besrd this emigration
scheme discussed, and I noticed that planters and other
employers of labor were taking very complaisant views
cf the movement. Whilst they expressed regrets at any
loss of labor, they were content to be relieved of a dis
turbing political element to some extent; and at tbe same
time they felt confident of replacing emigrant freedmen
with European immigrant?. Walhalla, a beautiful town,
and a flourishing German colony in Oconee County, South
Carolina, la an illustration of bow tbe South hopes to
compensste for any loss of colored people. We can get
of colored laborers tvilk their familiet, what few may be
required to meet any present needs of labor in this coun
try, and I don't recommend any more say one thousand
or thereabouts, without awakening the slightest unpleas
ant feeling among the Southern white people.
Now in regard to the alleged shiftlessness or incompe
tency as laborers of the freedmen of the late slave States,
I would ask you who has produced tbe last great cotton
crop r.f over five million bales. It will hardly do to
credit the scions of the old planting white aristocracy
with a production surpassing the best days of slavery.
No, not quite, you must admit that the colored man of '
tbe cotton States, though shiftless sometimes like his
white brother, is at this day and as a free man a better
laborer than he ever was as a slave, and is so recognize
by the great majority of employers, notwithstanding1,
complaints in newspapers, originating chiefly in political
acerbities.
We can get a supply of colored laborers from South
Carolina, or even Louisiana without any risk of unfavor
ably affecting onr reciprocity treaty. And we ran also
get coolie Immigrants from India which I regard as of
more importance in respect to population, if we try; and
in pursuing this matter, we have less to fear from sup
posed British hindrances or American disfavor, than we
have from our own jealousies and want of cordial co
operation.
But Mr. Editor, I do not venture into print at this
time, either in your Journal or In tbe columns of your
neighbor, for the sake of discussing Negro, Hindoo, or
any other immigration, except as in connection with the
great question of the equalization of the sexes. I am
slightly interested in the labor question, and only take
part In its discussion so far as it is connected with the
welfare of the country. But I write now and would raise
my voice on any and every suitable occasion, to fix the
attention of the King and people of the Hawaiian Islands
upon the injurious disproportion of tbe sexes in our pop
ulation.
In our chief industrial centres, where population is
grouped, there are two and in some plsces three adult

The "War in Africa.
Tbe causes wiich led to tbe serious war which
now exists between tbe English and the Zulus
in Africa, and which has latterly been so disastrous
to the forces of tbe former, have been but little
understood by the world at large. But the Wed
minster Jlevitw, (a liberal periodical) furnishes some
information on tbf subject which will be interesting.
The war grew oa of incidents to tbe annexation of
the Transvaal rerablic to the British South African
Colonies. It wa found, after this event, that a
strip of the anttxed territory had alwsys belonged
to tbe Zulus. The Colonial Government (Cape
Colony) arbitrated with tbe Zulu King, whose name
the Wtdminster Review gives as KaUhwayo, and
sot Cetywayo, as (ommonly printed and the arbi
tratora decided lbs tbe disputed land did belong; to
the Zala Kingdom Bat Sir Btrtle Frcre, the Gov
ernor of Cape Cohny, interposed conditions which
KaUhwayo could not accept without becoming a
vassal of England, ud be declined them. The con
ditions were that th) Zulus should pay a fine of 600
oxen, surrender sone captives, pay damsges to tbe
Boers, whom he had despoiled before the annexation,
disband bis army, take back the expelled missiona
ries, and worst of all, allow an agent of Eogland to
reside in Zululanc. with supreme power to asttle all
disputes that ma arise between the Zulus and any
Euroreao, binding Katshwsyo not to expel any
European witbott the consent of such British agent.
He was also lequired to give up the only seaport
from which the Zulus can obtain arms and ammuni
tion, St. Lucie boy. The hard terms were promptly
rejected, and Katshwsyo ordered the English to
evacuate Fort Luneberg, which is on tbe disputed
territory. Th'3 was refused, and the Zulus at once
went to war, s far with marked success. It appears
that the Zulu army is 42,000 strong, well disci
plined, compose! of troops who have been in service
constantly for oany year?, and armed with modern
muskets and artilery, both ol wbiea they know now
to use. The English bare but 4000 to oppose them,
and there is no reliance upon the native tribes in
CaDe Coloov. wbt are all in dread of the warlike
Zulus.
The British Parliament has voted 87.500,000 as a
first installment fo the conduct of the war. But
it is likely to cost them ten times as much and a
great loss of life before Katsbwayo is sunjugaiea, as
his country is next to impenetrable by European
troops, and transportation from the colonial towns
most expensive. It 1875 tbe European or white
population of Cape Colony was 236,000. It may
now be 200,000, anc might be able to raise an army
of 20,000 men. Ths finances of the colony are not
the best. The debt 8 $25,000,000, tbe expenditures
(1877) SI 7.225,000. and the revenue $13,150,000.
England will have ta supply most of the money and

BEAUTIFUL EYES OF BLUE.
O beautiful speaking eyes of blue!
Where did you oatch^chat glorious hue?
Have you sipped from the violet’s purple
brim.
Hidden away in the forest dim,
’Neath the cooling eaves
Of dark green leaves,
Where the singing brook its foam veil
weaves?
O beautiful sparkling eyes of blue!
Have you kissed the drops of pearly dew?
Or how did you come by that shining light,
When the hours of day have taken flight?
From the dews I wi6.
You 6tole a kiss.
Where the roses glowed in dewy bliss.
O beautiful starry eyes of blue!
Have you robbed the earth and heavens too?
Have you wooed the bride of the moonlit sky
Where the gems of night take wings and fly?
Oh, you look so true
Sweet eyes of blue.
But you’ve robbed the earth and heavens too.
—Yankee Blade.
(Mllfi A COOLIE.
It was in the eighties—I forget the
exact date—that I was an able seaman
on board the ship John E. Redwood, of
Phillipsbnrg, engaged in the East India
trade. This was my first deep water
voyage. Before this I had been in west
ern ocean vessels. During the dog
watches my mind was filled with the
wonders to be seen in a deep waterman.
I heard many yarns about the marvelous
tncks m juggling ol the natives of the
countries we visited, and of the fairly
desperate agility of the thieves that in
fested the seaport towns of India and
China.
After an uneventful voyage we ar
rived at Bombay, and one of the crew
having been chosen for night watchman
the rest of us were employed in working
cargo. The watchman’s duty consisted
in keeping a vigilant watch from 6 in the
evening until 6 next morning. He was
responsible for everything that was
stolen. The rest of the time he was al
lowed to do as he pleased.
My chum Bill Davis was chosen for
this duty, and we rather envied him.
To see him sitting down in the shade
smoking his pipe, while we were work
ing our soul cases off under a broiling
sun and with scarcely a breath of wind
to stir the air, was enough to make any
one envious.
One Saturday afternoon Bill came to
me as I was taking a quiet smoke and
asked me to stand his watch that night.
As Bill had been a good shipmate I
could not refuse him, although I was
dead tired. All hands except the cap
tain, the mate and myself, left for the
beach, bound on a grand lark.
Tt__ -Ain j_t_a _ 11 .
aw iihu uvui. uurjaxguuy wuv V T OU uiir.n
the ship seemed lonely and deserted.
The captain and mate were sitting on
the poop abaft the after house, having a
game and a smoke. I lingered around
the booby hatch and thought of the good
times ashoce and felt loqelier every min
ute.
After sunset there was scarcely any
twilight, for the change between day
and night was almost instantaneous.
What little breeze had been blowing
throughout the day had died out, and
the sea was like an immense mirror.
The sky was cloudless, and it was one
of those perfect nights that are only
seen in small latitudes. The men-of
„war started drilling with their search
lights, and the sight, as the light fell on
some stately ship, making her stand out
in bold relief while the rest of the fleet
was-an indistinguishable mass of shad
ows, was one never to be forgotten.
Watching the various doings in the har
bor served to pass the time, and I soon
forgot my surroundings, so absorbed did
I become in the different things that
were going on. Nothing disturbed the
stillness but now and then a boatload
of drunken firemen going off to their
vessel and disputing with their boat
man. Occasionally some one would
start a song, and as it drifted over the
water its harshness was lost and only its
beauty remained.
One by one these sounds died away,
and as there was nothing left to divert
my thoughts they came back to myself
and the ship. The silence was oppress
ive. I felt insignificant in the midst of
it. How small I was! My mind was
uneasy and restive. In fact, I was nerv
ous, and I could not account for it. In
order to calm myself as well as to kill
time I began walking up and down the
poop; Dm Having woraea nara au aay
I was soon fagged, and began hunting
around for something that would occu
py me. In my wanderings I found two
or three sheets of a New York newspa
per. This was a prize. I rigged the bin
nacle lamp in thewheelhouse, and fixing
myself comfortably in the captain’s chair
I crowded on all sail for intellectual en
joyment.
The only thing I could find was stock
reports, advertisements and shipping
news. This was rather of a disappoint
ment, but I started in to read those. I
found some of them quite interesting,
and presentiy I was taking solid com
fort in reading what I felt to be a spe
cies of news—dry as it was—from
home.
The door in front of me was open,
and the moon had come up full. Every
thing in its direct rays was bathed in
the brightest light, but the shadows
were horribly dark. I happened to
glance up as I puzzled over a queerly
worded notice, and my eye caught, for a
second only, the shadow of the head and
shoulders of a coolie. As I saw it, there
flashed through my mind the yams that
I had heard about the coolies stripping
themselves, then oiling their bodies and
swimming off to vessels with their
“dhu” or daggers; plundering the un
guarded' crews and disemboweling all
who tried to seize them as they slipped
through their hands. I jumped for the
deck, sheathknife in hand.
When I got on deck there was no one
in sight, and I listened for some sound,
but all was as quiet as a deep under
ground cell. It was as though both of
us were even holding our breath so that
we should not betray our whereabouts.
There was not even the lapping of waves
against the ship’s sides. As I started to
sneak to the after part of the wheel
house 1 could hear my heart beat, agjj
\
the twtmd of my footsteps as my bare
feet lightly touched the deck. When I
reached the corner of the wheelhouse I
brought my knife down around the cor
ner to the full extent of my arm. Not
feeling anything I ventured to look
around the corner. Not seeing any one
I turned the corner, and in this way I
proceeded around the house, carefully
knifing around each comer before tunn
ing it.
After having made the round of the
wheelhouse, I doubled on my track and
Went back the other way; but I could
Bee no sign of the presence of any one,
nor had I heard any noise. So after
searching the decks, forecastle and for
ward house, I concluded that whoever I
had seen must have slipped overboard
and escaped, or my imagination had
played me a trick. I finally brought to
again in the chair and began to read
once more, hut I had somehow lost in
terest and felt nervous. Every little
while I got up and made the tour of the
deck.
I had hardly settled myself after one
of these tours when I was startled by a
suppressed groan from the captain’s
room, followed by gasps, as if for
breath. These were succeeded by a
strange gurgling sound. My blood ran
cold, and for a minute I was paralyzed.
Then I understood it all. Instead of the
coolie going overboard he had descended
into the after cabin. While gathering
together plunder he had awakened the
captain. Then to save himself he had
cut the captain’s throat, which account
ed for the noise.
To preserve myself it became neces
sary for me to either secure this coolie
or to kill him, and as it would doubtless
be easier to kill him than to try to se
cure him, i sneaked out or the wheel
house to take a look around. I carefully
studied the ground, in order to decide
upon the best place for me to take up
my station. I finally fixed on the com
panionway. Noiselessly I crawled on
top of the house and knelt on the com
panionway slide. With my knife raised
ready for striking, I awaited the com
ing up of the coolie and murderer. I
had decided that it would be best to
stick the knife into his brain or along
side one of the big arteries in his neck.
I anxiously waited, with every nerve
strained, to detect his first approach,
every muscle tense and ready for a
quick and strong attack. Cramps in my
legs seized me, but I did not dare to
move, afraid each moment that he
would appear.
While in this position, and while
every sense was on the alert, I was
startled by a movement and a groan be
hind me. I turned with an involuntary
cry, not knowing what would confront
me—but I saw nothing.
By this time I was so scared I was un
able to think for a moment or two.
After collecting my senses I knew that,
although the sound seemed to have been
right back of me, it must have come
from the mate’s room in the forward
part of the house. As there were two
ways of getting down into the after
house, I was puzzled as to which one to
guara. x nnany ueuiueu uo ciuse me
after companionway and take my sta
tion at the watohhouse, which was the
only other way by which the coolie
could get out. If he came up the com
panionway I should hear him, and be
able to reach him before he slipped
overboard.
Sneaking along the alleyway I took up
my position alongside the watchhouse
door, and my senses being strained to
the utmost by this time I could faintly
hear some one moving about down be
low. I was worked up to an awful
pitch of excitement, in fact my muscles
had been strained so long that I trem
bled as with ague. My nerves were at
the breaking point.
How long I stood there I do not know.
I finally got so worked up that I could
hardly stand. I came to the conclusion
that if the coolie should come up I was
then too weak to offer any resistance,
and that if something didn’t happen
soon I should lose my mind. I concluded
that I could stand the strain no longer.
Carefully making my way to the rail I
broke down. I became afraid—afraid
even to go on the main deck and into
the deep shadows.
I was afraid to stand still; I kept look
ing over my shoulder and turning
around, not knowing where I should be
attacked or from what point. My mind
was getting unbalanced under the awful
pressure. To save myself I walked the
topgallant rail to the forecastle. From
there I went to the flying jib boom pole
facing inboard. My mind was made up
iu jump uvuiuutuu xx aiij' uuuy ttieu tu
come out after me. I sat there the rest
of that night, knowing I should be ac
cused of murdering these men, but I
came to the conclusion that it was bet
ter to stand a trial for double murder
than to become a maniac by watching
at that watchhouse door.
While awaiting daylight I could see
myself accused of murder and every
body laughing at my defense. I could
see myself bung in a foreign country.
After a long time I gathered what lit
tle courage I had left and came back to
the poop and carefully searched all
nooks and comers, but I did not dare to
go down below until the moon had set.
Then I noiselessly sneaked below. To
my surprise I found the mate peaceful
ly snoring in his bunk. This added con
siderably to my courage. Then I list
ened at the door of the captain’s room.
I distinctly heard him breathe. This
was an immense relief. I tried to think
it over. The only way in which I could
work it out was this—either my mind
had played me a trick or I had really
seen a coolie’s shadow, and, alarmed by
my movements, he had slipped over
board before securing his booty. Cer
tainly we never missed anything, and the
captain and the mate had only mumbled
or groaned in their sleep.—Lieutenant
J. H. Scott, U. S. R. M., in Romance,
Too Clover by Half.

A Republican state Convention
WILE BE HELD IN
City Hall, Portland,
TUESDAY, JDNE 21, 1892,
At 11 O'clock. A. M.,
for the purpose of nominating a candidate for
Governor, to he supported at the September
election; and nominating candidates tor electors
at large for President and Vice President, to he
supported at the November election and trans
acting any other business that may properly
come before it.
The basis of representation will bo as fol
lows: .
Each city, town and plantation will be en
titled to one delegate. and for each 76 votes
cast for the Republican candidate for Governor
in 1888, an additional delegate, and for a frac
tion of 40 votes in excess of 76 votes, an addi
tional delegate.
Vacancies in the delegation of any city, town
or plantation can only be filled by residents of
the county in which tlie vacancy exists.
Tlie State Committee will be in session iu tlie
reception room of the hall at 9 o’clock on the
morning of the convention, for the purpose of
receiving the credentials of delegates. Dele
gates in order to be eligible to participate in tlie
convention, must be elected subsequent to the
date of call for this convention.
All electors of Maine, without regard to past
political dilferences who are in favor of main
taining a protective tariff, who believe iu tlie
Republican poliev of reciprocity; in protecting
the Industries and the agricultural products of
this state; in elevating and dignifying human
labor by securing to the laborer his Just
reward;ill a sound and honest currency; in
free, popular education; in securing political
power to every citizen; in tlie promotion of the
cause of temperance;in just and equal taxation;
In all measures for the encouragement of Amer
ican sliip building, ana in an economical and
efficient administration of state affairs, are cor
dially invited to unite with the Republicans in
the selection of delegates to this convention.
Rer order Republican State Committee.
J. H. MANLEY, Chairman.
F. E. SOUTHARD, Secretory.
Augusta. Me.. May 16, 1892.

English Telegraphs.
The telegraphs in England are under the
control of the Post-office Department, and
the Postmaster General, in his recent re
port, devotes considerable space to them.
The total number of telegraph offices in the
Kingdom is 6,400, an increase of 306 dur
ing the year 1872. There were sent, dur
ing that year, nearly 16,000,000 of ordi
nary measagea, an increase of nearly three
millions over the previous year. A still
greater increase appeared in the number of
words transmitted for the newspapers,
over twenty-eight millions of words having
been sent for press messages, an increase
of seven millions of words. It is stated
that during a single night, when important
ministerial statements have been made in
Parliament, over two hundred thousand
words have been transmitted from the cen
tral telegraphic station in London.for pub
lication in the provincial newspapers. On
Kew Year's Day last, there were 106,000
miles of postal telegraphs in operation, of
which about five thousand miles were
rented by private persons. These private
wire# yielded the Government sn anualy
rental of $190,000. There are oyer 7,600
instruments in use in the English tele
graphic systems.

tain R B. Forties, of Boston. Mass ..states
that the compass in irou ships in specially
affected in certain localities on the coast
of Nova Scotia, which accounts for the
loss of steamers in that region. He fur
ther says that, in spite of corrections, ap
plied iu England, whereby iron ships may
be safely navigated m a given course ap
proximately west-south-west and east
north-east, when they come to head more
to the north or south by several |siints on
the American coast, their corrections, good
on the coast of England, are valueless in
some some ships. It is well known that
the heeling of the iron ship, the rolling,

- hence, nothing but constant observations

THE Blih>p Dr. -Marry, nays Let .Uonilu,
lias recently demonstrated that the heart
acts like all mechanical motors in that the
frequency of the pulsations vanes accord
ing to the resistance which it meets in
driving the blood through the vessels.
W hen the resistance becomes greater, the
throbs diminish ; they accelerate, on the
contrary, if the opjsiaitioti becomes less
During life, the action of the nervous cen
ters makes rself felt on the heart,of which
it renders the pulsations sinner or quicker,
whatever may be the resistance experi
enced. Dr Marey eliminated this uervoiis
intinence by removing the heart of an ani
mal, and causing it to work under purely
mecl inical conditions. The heart of a
turtle was arranged with a system of rub
ber tubes representing veins and arteries.
Calf's blood, deti bn Hated, w as caused to
circulate, and a registering instrument
noted the amplitude and frequency of the
movements of the organ When the tube
containing ihr biota) leawng the heart was
compressed, the liquid accumulated in rear
of the obstacle ami the heart emptied it
self with greater difficulty, the pulsations
weakening perceptibly, tin relaxing the
pressure, thus allowing free course to the
blood, the throbs accelerated rapidly.

T.1ID BURSTING BOMBS
AND ROCKETS' GLARE
Heroism of Two Red Cross Doc
tors and Two Women Canteen
Workers During Bombardment.
Inst outside tbe French town of
Oomplegn stands the Chateau of An-,
nel, an ancient structure dating back;
hundreds of rears. Its walls and yault
ed rooms could tell many a tale of
caedlaeral adrenture perchance of
violence of romance and sorrow.
Today It can tell the story of two
surgeons American Red Cross doc
tors who for a day and a night and a
second day eared hundreds of lives
operating by candle light upon wound
ed French soldiers, the while shells
crashed and the ping of machine gun
bullets resounded the while the ene
my was advancing steadily until he got
within five miles of the little hospital.
The chateau had been used as an
advance hospital under direction of
the French Service de Sante. Major
Tarnowsky and Major Morehead, two
Red Cross doctors, were In charge,
the personnel being French. When
the enemy came within six kilometers
the French authorities ordered the
evacuation of the place, the patients
being removed at night In canal boats.
The two Red Cross doctors were at
their wits ends. Their labors were
circumscribed by human limitations.
There were Just four hands and no as
sistance of any kind. But somehow
fate usually intervenes when the need
Is direct So In the nick of time two
American canteen women came over
from Complegne.
Still, supplies were lacking, and
again fate proved kind. Five heavily
laden Red Cross camions arrived with
In an hour after the women, each with
itwo men, so that the personnel cow
consisted of two doctors, two women
and ten men. '
; Inside two begrimed human beings,:
.spattered with blood, tenpe of face.,!ut'
alert and ready for any emtfency.,
Two brave women are hanging them;
the Instruments as thj ure needed j
the bandages, the otLer surgical para-1
phernalla. Other men are holding can
dles, administering anaesthetics, car
rying In thQ wounded, carrying out
those whose wounds have been bound
np, playing hospital angels In an un
known tongue to the fellows lying In
the wards.
CONDENSED MILK FOR
LITTLE SICILIANS
The problem of caring for the babies
of Sicily was one which the Red Cross
workers were almost at a loss to solve
until the shipment of sweetened con
densed milk came to their rescue. A
can of condensed milk would last a
baby a week, for one spoonful, put In a
cup of boiling water, was sufficient for
a feeding, and so, to many a Sicilian
family the terms "America" and "con
densed milk" will always be synony
mous. Last February a list of fifty families
of Italian soldiers was given to the
Red Cross workers In Italy. The fam
ilies were carefully Investigated and
registered and the milk was given to
them for the little children, especially
the babies. Mothers came for the cans
and were grateful beyond expression.
But with the coming of warm
weather It was necessary to give out
the milk dally, as It would not keep
sweet. The mothers brought their
own containers, and the milk was pre
pared, sugar added, and they were In
structed carefully as to Its use. Abso
lute cleanliness of receptacles was In
sisted upon, and the homes were vis
ited In order to make sure that the
milk rations were being backed by
healthful conditions.
Aside from the saving of the lives of
the children, the soldiers are happier
and they are better soldiers now that
they know that their loved ones are
being cared for by the Crossa Rossa.
FOOD AT LOW COST
FOR BELGIAN FAMILIES
Through the combined efforts of the
American Red Cross and the Belgian
Government, food Is now supplied at
low cost to the families of those Bel
gian soldiers who are at the front or
have been killed or taken prisoner.
The Commissioner for Belgium of the
Red Cross recently advised the Bel,
glan Government that the American
people were ready to undertake this
work and that the Red Cross would
provide 20,000 francs a month for it
The Belgian Minister of Supplies
has a chain of sixty-five stores in
France and at the front, which sell
goods to the families of Belgian sol
diers working In munition plants or
other places in the rear.
FAITH IN THE RED CROSS.
A young American soldier recently
arrived In France tried to locate his
brother, who was also In the army
and who had preceded him "over
there." It was like hunting for a
needle In a haystack. After many dif
ficulties and long waiting, however, he
at last located the company to which
his brother belonged.
. Then he learned that his brother
had been wounded and was In some
hospital. That was all he could find
out With the faith of a little child
propose in his eyes he told this to a hospital

RED GROSS IS LIKE
A SHIP OF RESCUE
Always Travels In Troubled
Waters and Answers Every
Cry of Distress.
REST OF WORLD IS TOO BUSY
Christmas Roll Call Gives Every One
a Chance to Take Part in
Rebuilding Our Broken
World.
The American Red Cross Is perhaps
like nothing so much as a stanch and
loyal ship In a storm. It goes its way
with senses tuned to catch nny cry for
help. And when that cry comes, It
drives Instantly and without fear
straight to thf place of distress, in
flood and fire and disaster, just as the
ship braves the perils of tumbling
seas and hazardous rescue work. And,
again, like the ship, it STANDS BY
till those endangered are helped to
safety.
Meanwhile the rest of the world,
busy with Its own problems, hurries
home during these times of storm and
stress, and draws down the blinds.
At least vthat Is the way It has been
In the past. But now comes the Christ
mas Roll Call. And it is a privilege,
not a pest. It has no preferences. It
plays no favorites. It makes no ex
ceptions. It summons every man,
woman and child in the country. It
holds out to each one the blessed op
portunity to ride on every Red Cross
ship of mercy, to speed with every
Red Cross train of relief that encir
cles the earth on their errands of
mercy.
The only wny for anyone to escape
the possibility of some time having to
accept CHARITY from the Red Crosi
is to become ONK with the Red Cross.
For terrible calamity may come to us
all. The money wealth of the Belgians
was as nothing when they were stripped
of clothing and food. And that feel
ing of oneness with the organization
that our men on the other side have
had during the war was not merely a
great, but was the GREATEST, factor
In enabling the Red Cross to give the
efficient aid that It did.
Let us remember what Mrs. Mar
garet Lalng, canteen worker in France,
told about our boys who came out of
the hospitals without money :
"Sometimes they would be able to
make up a few cents between them,M
she said, "and sometimes they did not
have anything. They would hang be
hind those who could pay. And they
would look at the food so wistfully
that It made one fight back the tears.
The only wny we could get them to
take what they needed and craved was
by saying: 'You know, boys, this was
all paid for by your own people at
home.' Then immediately their atti
tude would change and they would
say : 'Why, yes, my mother' or 4my sls
ter gives to the Red Cross. And ther
how they would pitch In."
We are proud, we Americans. We
do not want something for nothing;
And here is our glorious opportunity
to take the rest of our nation by th
hand, and with all pride and dignity In
sure ourselves of our own help in tlm
of adversity.
This Christmas Roll Call gives every
one a chance to be a "Dollar Man.'
And most of us can be one right al
home. For by Joining the Red Crosi
now and paying the dollar we becom
as actively engaged in the great worh
as if we were giving all of our tim
to It. We are merely making our dol
lar substitute for those of us who nr
too busy to give nil of our time to th
Red Cross.
Some of the great achievements o
the Red Cross have been told over an
over, until the facts may seem old te
you. But on this occasion they an
worth telling again. We should not
forget, for Instance, how the womet
of this nation, like our first Colonial
mothers, turned ' suddenly into greal
manufacturers and made garments an?
supplies worth $f.0,000,000 last year
Nor let us forget how $111,000,000 wa
sent into the devastated countries dun
Ing the time while men and women,
giving their time for nothing, went
with those dollars to see that thej
were u$ed In the way they were most
needed. And the American Red Crosi
sent medicines and anesthetics to the
hospitals of France when they wer
almost unobtainable, so that our boyf
and their allies might have some re
lief from the torment of their wounds
and a chance at ultimate recovery.
There are so many things to tell
that It Is Impossible to spread th
whole story In this limited space. 'But
each worker will know. For the let
ters that have come from the boys iq
the camps "over here" and from the
fields "over there" have been full ol
the reasons. Ask the mother of any
boy who wa,s imprisoned behind the
cruel lines where food was scarce even
for the enemy army, but who got his
20 pounds of biscuits, pork and beans,
cocoa and other good, wholesome
things, every week.
The roofs are at hand everywhere
The reasons are manifest. Everyone
' should become a member of the widest,
hest and holiest crusade the world
COCKEREL OUTOINT3
TIC&R BY DODGING
Animal Loose In I Baggage Car Un
able to Land Paw cn
Crowifcg Fowl.
Calcutta. Grtat excitement was
caused at the lojal railroad terminus
a few days ago, when, on the arriv
al of the Madra.1 mall train, It became
known that a full-grown Bengal tiger
had broken loose from Its cage In tho
baggage car at the end of the train.
T!ie tigor a magnificent specimen
was part cf a consignment sent by
the Maharajah of Mysore as a gift to
the Calcutta Zoo, the other animals
being two llamas and six kangaroos, a
cockerel and two hens, the tiger hav
ing a cage to himself.
A coolie entered the compartment
adjoining tho cages on the train's ar
rival at Calcutta, and saw that the
tlgor had broken out of its cage and
entered the cage In which the llamas
and poultry were confined. The
coolie ran to obtain assistance, and
soon a large crowd gathered at a
respectful distance on a railway
bridge.
Zoo and railway officials who an
swered witnessed a remarkable spec
tacle. It was found that the tiger had
already killed a llama and the two
hens, but had failed to vanquish the
cockerel, which was' still walking
about freely, having successfully kept
its opponent at bay by mean9 of a
sort of "fowl jlu Jitsu."
The tiger, again and again, tried Its
utmost to land its paw on the cockerel
but the latter cleverly evaded all
blows aimed at it, crowing triumph
antly after the end of each round.
.The car was finally detached from
the train and removed to the freight
shed by the railway authorities, and
after several hours some of the iron
liars of the car were cut away and a
new cage placed against the opening.
It wa not until a bucket of water
had been placed in the new cage, how
ever, that the now thirsty tiger was
induced to get into it. A porter eye
witness of the astonishing light be
tween the tiger and the cockerel
stated that "if everyone was afraid of
the tiger, the tiger was afraid of the
cock."
The cockerel, after the removal of
the tiger, cooly hopped out of the
luggage van without so much as a
scra'ch.

1 __ I ARRY, just jog over to Uncle Bob'a,
f"I I 'will you, and tell hita to come
1,,,,,] here tomorrow? I've got a tiger for
WffiA him, I think. At all events, we'll
'tBISUUf have ag 0 n t the pigs." ' ,
, . Harry's eyes sparkled at the hints
given by the words "tiger" and "Uncle Hob."
This gentleman had earned the reputation in
.Singapore of a "terror" among the tigers. He
carried a rare cool head, and was among the
best of the crack shots, while Harry was full
of hunting ambition. .•-• : . /':'■
■' "No hurry, are you, father?" responded the
eager sportsman. ' . -
"Not that I know of. Why do you ask?"
"Well, I thought I'd tramp across through
the jungle with my Bhot-gun, and bag a few
pheasants, if time is no object. I'll stop at
Uncle Bob's over night, if I am belated.".'
"Well, Harry, I should say rifle; butHt's
your risk, you know. Look sharp after your
self." . \ -.
"I've never seen anything between this and
Uncle Bob's worse than a stray pig now mid
then," replied the lad. "I can't risk Sing
with the rifle, and I want the birds."
"Do as you think best, my boy, but I should
«ay rifle,' responded Mr. Carnie, as he turn
ed toward the house. .
Royal Carnie was a silk buyer for a great
German commercial house, and passed con
siderable time at his country plate, near his
brother Bob's tea plantation. He, as well as
Bob. was a mighty hunter, and there wm
much game in the neighborhood. ■ .
Harry hastily prepared for his tramp
through the woods. It was eight or ten miles
to Uncle Bob's by the route he had decided
upon. I
1 Ordinarily he would have galloped over on
his pony, but just now he felt an appetite for
birds. <"• ■<■': j .
Sing, a Chinese coolie, who was Harry's
constant attendant, raced eagerly after the
double-barrel at his young master s order. He
was very fond of accompanying Harry in his
"hunting expeditions. He was a great coward
himself, as a general thing, though brave in
spurts when inspired by devotion to Harry,
and he possessed unbounded confidence in
the daring and skill of his young master. - •
The double-barrel was a prime old-fashioned
muzzle-loader, which had done much good
service. One barrel was charged with shot,
and the other with a bullet. ,
Harry did not really anticipate any great
amount of sport, but he could not resist the
temptation of beating the forest and jungle,
where he had been so often, just to see "what
was going on," and the shot-gun would -be
handy to knock over any stray specimen of
the feathered tube for supper. . - -
They were soon off, King carrying a haver
sack with just a bite for tiffin in it, and Har
ry sporting the double-barrel and hia hunting
knife. I
Sing also bore the inevitable bamboo pole,
without which no reputable coolie ever stirred
into the forest. -
They had proceeded tome four or five miles
1
BOYS AND GIRLS MAGAZINE SECTION
without encountering anything that begged
for the shot-gun, wben-Harry^ keen -.ear de
tected a suspicious sound in the distance
"Hi, boy! Hist!" he cried, softly, to Sing,
and halted to listen., '•',■" ■
After a brief interval of silence, the' noise
was again borne faintly' to his ears from the
distance. . , - . '
It seemed like a confused murmur of
shrieks and cries. Harry could not make it
out. . ■ .-.-■ .
"What is it, Sing?" he whispered. ' '
Sing had his ear to the ground, and was
listening intently.
"Wolf, 'Master Harry, I think," he replied,
with his eyes gleaming. "Heap tight over
there!" ' :' : ■■ ■ , > -..
Now. the cries were heard again,-- and this
time more distinctly. ■ . .:. . , • ." i ■'•;
"Heap fight!" echoed Sing, with a mani
fest disposition to turn back on . the trail, j
, "Come on, you rascal!" cried. Hal, "lets
see what nißchief ib up." ' m
And he set out in the direction of the mys
terious clamor, with Sing shuffling 'behind.
As they progressed, • the tumult began to
swell more distinctly. 1 They could distin
guish notes from several throats. .■ •, 'A ' savage
chorus of some sort was in process of lively
execution, and it sounded quite unlike joy.
Harry began to advance with more cau
tion. There was something in the -wild clam
or that was' very ~ mysterious to his ears,
though he,.inclined to Sing's opinion that it
was wolvea, who were having a 'heap ngnt
over some sort of booty. Yet till the real
danger .was clearly known, it was well to be
They ". continued to , progress through the
thickets with 'as noiseless tread as possible
till they came to a • low ridge covered with
trees, tangled with vines.; and a" scattering
undergrowth which greatly helped them to
advance without being ■seen.--"' ■ ' , . - ;■ _
They crossed the ridge, and then the wild
chorus rang out distinct, and clear. | Yelps,
screeches and howls of anguish were mingled
with angry snarls and prolonged whines in a
manner calculated to make the hair, of the
casual listener straighten up in a hurry.
"Oho!" exclaimed Harry/ "here s a circus
of the biggest size, I suspect. r*ow, bing,
you just keep your wits about you and come
With the utmost precaution they crept for
ward, Hal's eyes sharply scanning every copse
and bush for a lurking foe, while poor 6ing
shivered like an aspen, and clutched his bam
boo as if it were his last straw. He kept
close to his master's heels, and hia eyes rolled
from side to side, anil glared up into the
vines and tree* in the wildest terror. ; '•• .
■At length they .were 'close upon.-. the scene
of the tumult. They stopped to listen. The
yelping -was plaiulß^thut •of ■ wolves, possibly
in combat among themselves. -'•■.; ■
. Hut no! there arose a savage cry that made
the tiesh ' creep. ■■•■■■
"My goodness!" exclaimed Harry,'stealth
ily hastening forward, and peering through
the Vines and shrubs.
Suddenly he paused and raised a warning
hand to bing.
"There's fun for you! he said.
- A serious expression came over his face.
while 'his eyes were fixed with a strange fas
cination upon the scene before him. --■'"
A huge fallen tree lay in a small area of
open glade, and upon a great projecting limb,
a few feet above the ground, was a magnifi
cent panther at bay.
She looked like . a demon of mischief, as
she stood there among the snaky lianas that
had dragged the forest monarch -to its death.
Her jaws were half open, displaying a grin
ning row of formidable teeth, and from her
throat ppured forth a torrent of vindictive
growls and snar's. - • • .- ' ■,-■.■■
One paw was lifted in an attitude of de
fense against a score of yelping and snapping
wolves, who were crowding about the tree,
laying - desperate siege to the savage queen
of .the jungle. ■ '
,' Two or three of the rascals were rolling
over in agony and wiping their bleeding
snouts, which had evidently formed too close
an acquaintance with the formidable paw, as
the result of some venturesome leaps at the
panther. ' "
"Oh, isn't she a beauty!" exclaimed Har
"ry, crouching behind a turf of reeds to enjoy
a leisurely view of " the exciting combat.
"-"Come home, master! Come home heap
quick!" urged Sing, anxiously, from behind
him.
"Keep quiet, you coward!" was Hal's only
response, as he intently watched the thrilling
scene before him. ■ . . . . ...
It was really/ not an uncommon one for an
Oriental forest, buf Hal, who bad recently
returned from his schooling in England, had
never happened to see it until now, and it
thoroughly fascinated him. ■ . -
In general the wolf has a dread of the pan
ther. No half-dozen of them would dare at
tack him, but a score or more, taking their
terrible enemy at -a disadvantage, .will some
times make an assault. ' f
The panther himself does not -seek a fight
with beasts of his own stamp. He prefers
the Umid gazelle, or other small animals, upon
which he can pounce unawares, and even birds
which he pursues up into the trees..
But he is an elegant animal in hia motions,
and clean and dainty in his habits like all the
family of • cats, - while the wolf is . in every
respect literally "a mean brute." '".(
Hal watched this strange biitt'e with intense
interest for several minutes. ■ The waving tail,
the arching back, the alert - movements -of
the waiehtul paw, all were -the very grace
of motion. . _:
Now and then, out of the howling mob of
wolves, a form would leap desperately at the
limb, only to be knocked yelling back by a
sharp and decisive sweep of that powerful
•The panther grew more and more furious,
a ii il more nervous in her movements. It is
quite | possibla. that she scented . Harry, , for
she - began to turn her head uneasily, and
showed symptoms of a dash at her beleaguer
ing foes. r '■ : ; ■*.•;,
Harry drew up -his gun.v ;- • . ' ,■ :
I "Shoot cat, master?". whispered .the treuv
bling King, ' " ■ ' -■*-•--. •" < * - . ■
• '.' Not : by a long sight!" replied Hal. "1
don't want any quarrel .with her just now.
Wait till I get my rifle.",' • -) ■.-
The shot-gun barked ' instantly ■ after this
remark, and one of the wolves, giving a con
vulsive leap,' staggered away . into the brush. .
. .At the same instant, the cat bounded with
a savage screech into the startled horde. Her
vengeful claws tore a passage "Tight and left
through the ranks of the demoralized enemy,
and she disappeared with marvelous leaps
into the jungle. ..., .'■■:-
The wolves, who utterly lost their heads at
this double assault, scattered wildly in groups
in different directions. One peculiarly large
and ; ferocious beast rushed off by himself,
aud us he took the direction of Uncle Bob*,
Harry hastily reloaded, ramming a bullet into
each barrel, and followed on his tracks.
. "Why you no shoot cat?" chattered Sing,
as he trotted on at Harry's heels.
"Why, 1 told you," I want to save her for
a good rifle," Hal replied. "I couldn't riik
this thing. Then she is really too handsome
to shoot when you . can get a pop at those
beastly wolves."
After ' proceeding about half a mile, the
hunters encountered a terrified Chinaman rac
ing a (.tosh a cultivated field. ' . ■ '
"Did you see a wolf pass here?" shouted
Harry. . "
"\es, yes! -Up there! just gone!" replied
the man, without relaxing his flight or look
ing around. . .^
: ' But Harry knew well enough what "up
there" meant. At the time of the .Tae-ping
rebellion, which was crushed by the English
General Gordon, the insurgents had cut the
hills into breastworks, and fortifications.
When anybody in that neighborhood said "up '
there," : he meant on those hills with the mil
itary relicß.' '
Harry hurried forward in that direction,
With his Chinese boy behind begging him to
- After going some distance he espied -the
wolf slinfting behind a clump of* bushes. He
quickly drew upon him, and the animal leap
ed out, evidently hit hard. - v --
Aft he appeared in the open, "Hal let fly .
with the other barrel. • This also was a hit,
for Harry was an excellent shot already, with
his comparatively brief practice.
This last wound excited the rage of the fe
rocious animal to that degree that he dashed
savagely at the hunter. Sing incontinently'
took to his heels with a yell of terror.'
What was to be done? The musket being
a muzzle-loader, there was no time . to re
charge. -»■-. — • ■ • ■ , ■
Hay struck an attitude of defense,, flour
ished his weapon as a bludgeon, and awaited
the assault. ...
The wolf came tearing on^ with an appall
ing display of his . ugly-looking teeth, till he
arrived at a distance of about twenty-Hvo _
feet. Then he seemed to think better, of it;'
or possibly hia strength failed him. • :>},:' :'~i~;
At all events/ he sat down upon bis haunch- ■
es and held a council of war. , ... • ■
& Horry remained cautiously on guard,', while
the terrified phiz of the fugitive bmg peered
anxiously out from ■ behind a ' rather remote
tree. ■ - - ■- ■■■■,'.-
After a little reflection, the wolf grinned fe
rociously at the expectant hunter, and with

SOLDIERS of fortune have woven
romances. Now comes the
etcnographer of fortune, click
ing out romance and adventure
In the Orient. She Is a typical Ameri
can girl with spirit and daring that
make foreigners criticise yet admire

You will find her In every port along

and Cairo, the route so well known
to those who have heard and obeyed
the "Kast a-calllng." It Includes Kobe
and Nagasaki, Shanghai, Hongkong,
Manila. Singapore, Colombo. In China
you will And her as far north as Pekln
and inland to Hankow.
Ai tin. height of tho revolution in
rhlna and the beginning of the siege
of Hankow an American firm in the
trading business in that city had its
monthly collections, amounting to thou
sands of dollars in cash and checks,
on hand. The city was practically cut
'.rf from outside communication and
business was at a etandstill. In view
nf a probable sacking of the city the
nly way to save the firm's money was
to get It to a bank In Shanghai.
When it was realized that all the
men of the company, members of the
Hankow volunteer corps, must stay to
aid in the protection of the foreign
settlement Miss Edna Lodge, the firm's
stenographer, volunteered to make the
trip. When she declared that for her
own protection she would try to reach
Shanghai anyway the memljcra of the
llrm gave in and agreed to send her
with the money.
With one ot the Chinese compradors
and a coolie from the office whom they
believed they could trust they started
her with $6,000 in currency and about
tu nnd tn xhcckn nn what at best
would be a three or four day trip down
the Yangste-klang,
In the company's launch they
travelled a day and a half down the
river, successfully evading the rebel
soldiers who were watching the river.
Then something went wrong with the
engines, and when he failed to make re
pairs after an hour or so tinkering the
panic stricken comprador fled. He did
not neglect, however, to tako a service
had with them, and the
stenographer was left with u stupid
loollo and a little ,n cauoro revolver
i ,t Vi ii f nu'n tn nriitpct her.
As she said afterward she simply
bullied the coollo into going on iuoi
with her a few miles to the nearest
m,,i rifar i'Mlnir. wliprn thev managed
to get ponies of the huwJI wiry Chi
nese breed. On these they headed for
Nankin, wnere she Knew tney coum
count on the railroad line being still

without taking a supply of foreign

Yokohama Is ultra English In social
atmosphere. Out of the comparatively
small community of foreigners the guest
list to social functions on the IllurT,
the beautiful residential section back
of the city, Is rigidly made up. Stenog
raphers usually are not on the list,
but one, a stenographer In one ot the
banks, was made nn exception. Six
months after she hud been transferred
from the Manila branch of the concern
a burst of temper led to the discovery
of systematic stealing that had been
going on in the bank for eight months.
Like the majority ot firms In Japan
and China, tho bank employed com
pradors, Chinamen who could be
trusted in the handling of money. One
In particular, un old man, shuffled
nround with an air of Oriental indiffer
ence that grated on the American girl
from Manila.
One day in her work she found it
necessary to have one ot the account
books from the vaults in order to copy
some entries. She sent one of the
Jupancse clerks to ask the aged com
prador to bring It. After watting some
little while and the book not appearing
she sent again. She sent a third re
quest, which met with equal Indiffer
ence.
Irritated, she went to the Inner rooms
to get It herself. She met the China'
man going Into one of the vaults with
a tray of coins in paper wrappings.
When to her fourth request he merely
affected not to hear she lost her temper
and seizing him by the arm gave him
u good shaking. The combination of
this and his astonishment tipped the
tray and several rolls of coins dropped
to the cement floor.
Tho paper on one roll burst. Out
rolled several fifty sen pieces and a lot
of one sen coppers, which are of the
same slzo as the sliver fifty sen pieces.
Iteallzlng the fraud In an Instant the
girl rushed to the cashier's office, while
the comprador fled the building. He
wus captured an hour later In the na
tive section of the city and confessed
that for months from time to time he
had been opening rolls of fifty sen pieces
stored as cash reserve In the vaults and
substituting tho one sen pieces. At the
ends of each roll he placed one or two
of the original silver pieces, and when
the rolls were superficially checked up
from time to time the fraud passed un
detected. He had managed to make
way with some eight thousand yen, all
of which had gone for gambling,
Tho comprador was sent to Neglshl
prison, while tho stenographer was
thanked by the manager, received a
raise In salary and was invited to his

AND ADVENTURE IN THE FAR EAST
It is a long way to bring stenogra
phers out from home tn the Orient on
contracts, with the result that the Amer
ican stenographers who have had cx-
How Daring Miss Edna
Money During Chinese
Pale of Society, Girls
perlenco In the Tar East always are in
demand. The fact that good stenog
raphers are few and far between led to
a feud between the managers of two
American firms in Toklo that now Is
history.
One man In urgent luid of a stenog
rapher had heard of a girl working for
firm in Kobe, a city COO miles south.
Her contract was about to terminate.
He got Into touch with her and made
arrangements to have lur come to To
klo and work for him.
At the club a few ev nings later he
happened to mention the uu ident to a
friend who was at the laail of the
agency of another ilrm. The other man,
ilso In need of a stenographer. Imme
diately telegraphed un uffir tu the
stenographer In Kobe that was so much '
better than the tlrst on.; that in view of
the fact that no contract hail 1 u
signed she accepted the second.
When the lirst man heaiil of It lie
said nothing, but a few nights later at
the cliilt when the second man was
iskul to take u vacant seat at u bridge
table he quickly arose and refused tu
play with his competitor. The story
later came out and for a year and it
half the feud was the talk of foreign
business circles In Toklo. Meanwhile
one of the members of tho llrm rep.
lescllteil by the seiontl mall rami! out
on a tour of Inspec tion, He hi aril of
the feu J and the cause of it, but said
nothing. However, six months later.
upon his return to the home office, the
matter was taken up there and led to
the recall and resignation of the Toklo
manager.
The year following the Husso-Japa-
nese war this same girl, now a veteran
unoiig Kastern stenographers of for
tune, accepted the offer of a place In
tho office of a machinery agency in
Vladivostok. L'pon her arrival ther.
she found the city topsy-turvy. Kvery-
body had money, the place was tilled
with adventurers, the drifters attracted
from all over the world by the cxdt -merit
and opportunities for speculation
and plunder in a business way that war
offers.
iiii feature typical of the times that
followed tho war was tho abundance
and common use of champagne. It had
been shipped in for sale to the llusslau
army. With the war ended tho sales
had dropped as the regiments were re
called and Vladivostok was lllled with
eusea of wine. It was turned over to
the local dealers and played a promi
nent part tn the night and day revels
that held sv ay In, the city.
The new stenographer, unable to find
room In tho crowded hotels or the few
boarding houses that she dared enter,
was forced to make her living quarters
In a room In tin. rear of the offices,
For protection she Umght a Husslan
wolfhound. Some weeks later, as she
was getting accustomed to her sur
rounding.", tho animal, which had be
come attached to her and slept near
her bed at, night, was the means of
saving her life.
Un tho middle ot the night the dng
rose, his hair bristling. He uttered low

line Yokohama girl was asked by a

Hut it's a saying in the Far Kast that

come homesick und go home at the end

and their return means that tho Orient

stenographers of fortune: they have
stayed the magic three years, and tho
Hast, with the glamour, side by side with
tilth ami ofttlmis discomforts, has
claimed tliem,

text:
We have arranged for a
series of six interesting articles,
"War Talks, by Uncle Dam,"
written by Mr. Howard H.
Gross, president of the Univer
sal Military Training League, of
which this is the first. They tell
In a graphic way why military
training Is of value, both to the
nation and to the individual, and
our readers will find them of
unusual interest.
--------------- --'- ------Y
WAR TALKS
By UNCLE DAN
Number One
America Must Fight Hard or Germany
May Win-Necessity for MIIl.
tary Training.
"Now, Billie," said his mother, "your
Uncle Dan is coming tomorrow to
spend a week with us on the farm, and
if you want to know about the war,
here's your opportunity. Uncle Dan is
probably one of the best-informed men
in the country." Billie clapped his
hands and gave such a whoop that he
wakened the baby, but what could you
expect of a fifteen-year-old boy who Is
a living interrogation point and wants
to know about war?
Uncle Dan arrived In due time and
Billie watched for an opportunity. It
came that evening after dinner when
Uncle Dan had lighted a cigar and
taken a seat on the porch.
'Tm mighty glad you came, Uncle
Dan. I want to talk to you about the
war. We have just put military train
ing in our township high school, but
we had a hard time to do It. The
Joneses and the Greggs objected. They
said the war wouldn't come over here.
Grandma Jones said: "They ain't no
use to worrit, it will soon blow over.'
Well, we put the training in just the
same. You orter heard Judge Brow
nell, the president of the school board,
do the slackers up. He said unless we
take off our coats and go to it, Ger
many may yet win, and if she does, she
will take over the great British fleet as
a war trophy and compel us to do what
ever she wants to; that she could make
us pay all the cost of the war; the
kaiser could tax us as he pleased and
that we couldn't help ourselves. He
could make every one pay over a part
of what he earns; that he could make
the farmers pay rent for their own
farms, etc. Now, Uncle, what do you
think of that?"
"Well, my boy," said Uncle Dan,
"all that Judge Brownell says might
easily come true and may unless we go ft
quickly to the aid of the allies with
large numbers of men and help them
break the German line. Unless we can I
beat the submarines, they may pre- s
vent us from getting enough food to y
the allies to keep them going. In that
case Germany would win. As matters "
stand today, our greatest need is a
trained men. If we had had several
millions of men with military training
in our Industries and on our farms iI
when the war came, who could S
have been called at once for service, I
do not believe the kaiser would have
forced the war upon us. As it was, he 1'

"There is only one safe way," said II
